as single operations, not as batch operations.

`db.operation.batch.size` SHOULD be set to the number of operations in the batch.
It SHOULD NOT be set for non-batch operations.

A request to execute a batch operation with no operations SHOULD also be treated
as a batch operation, and `db.operation.batch.size` SHOULD be set to `0`.

entities.name: db.operation.name
entities.brief: The name of the operation or command being executed.
entities.note:
It is RECOMMENDED to capture the value as provided by the application
without attempting to do any case normalization.

The operation name SHOULD NOT be extracted from `db.query.text`,
when the database system supports query text with multiple operations
in non-batch operations.

If spaces can occur in the operation name, multiple consecutive spaces
SHOULD be normalized to a single space.

For batch operations, if the individual operations are known to have the same operation name
then that operation name SHOULD be used prepended by `BATCH `,
otherwise `db.operation.name` SHOULD be `BATCH` or some other database
system specific term if more applicable.

entities.name: db.operation.parameter
entities.brief: A database operation parameter, with `<key>` being the parameter name, and the attribute value being a string representation of the parameter value.
entities.note:
For example, a client-side maximum number of rows to read from the database
MAY be recorded as the `db.operation.parameter.max_rows` attribute.

`db.query.text` parameters SHOULD be captured using `db.query.parameter.<key>`
instead of `db.operation.parameter.<key>`.

entities.name: db.oracledb.client
entities.brief: Spans representing calls to a Oracle SQL Database adhere to the general [Semantic Conventions for Database Client Spans](database-spans.md).
entities.note: `db.system.name` MUST be set to `"oracle.db"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

entities.name: db.postgresql
entities.brief: Attributes for PostgreSQL

entities.name: db.postgresql.client
entities.brief: Spans representing calls to a PostgreSQL database adhere to the general [Semantic Conventions for Database Client Spans](database-spans.md).
entities.note: `db.system.name` MUST be set to `"postgresql"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

entities.name: db.query.parameter
entities.brief: A database query parameter, with `<key>` being the parameter name, and the attribute value being a string representation of the parameter value.
entities.note:
If a query parameter has no name and instead is referenced only by index,
then `<key>` SHOULD be the 0-based index.

`db.query.parameter.<key>` SHOULD match
up with the parameterized placeholders present in `db.query.text`.

It is RECOMMENDED to capture the value as provided by the application
without attempting to do any case normalization.

`db.query.parameter.<key>` SHOULD NOT be captured on batch operations.

Examples:

- For a query `SELECT * FROM users where username =  %s` with the parameter `"jdoe"`,
  the attribute `db.query.parameter.0` SHOULD be set to `"jdoe"`.

- For a query `"SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %(userName)s;` with parameter
  `userName = "jdoe"`, the attribute `db.query.parameter.userName` SHOULD be set to `"jdoe"`.

entities.name: db.query.summary
entities.brief: Low cardinality summary of a database query.
entities.note:
The query summary describes a class of database queries and is useful
as a grouping key, especially when analyzing telemetry for database
calls involving complex queries.

Summary may be available to the instrumentation through
instrumentation hooks or other means. If it is not available, instrumentations
that support query parsing SHOULD generate a summary following
[Generating query summary](/docs/db/database-spans.md#generating-a-summary-of-the-query)
section.

For batch operations, if the individual operations are known to have the same query summary
then that query summary SHOULD be used prepended by `BATCH `,
otherwise `db.query.summary` SHOULD be `BATCH` or some other database
system specific term if more applicable.

entities.name: db.query.text
entities.brief: The database query being executed.
entities.note:
For sanitization see [Sanitization of `db.query.text`](/docs/db/database-spans.md#sanitization-of-dbquerytext).
For batch operations, if the individual operations are known to have the same query text then that query text SHOULD be used, otherwise all of the individual query texts SHOULD be concatenated with separator `; ` or some other database system specific separator if more applicable.
Parameterized query text SHOULD NOT be sanitized. Even though parameterized query text can potentially have sensitive data, by using a parameterized query the user is giving a strong signal that any sensitive data will be passed as parameter values, and the benefit to observability of capturing the static part of the query text by default outweighs the risk.

entities.name: db.redis
entities.brief: Attributes for Redis

entities.name: db.redis.client
entities.brief: Spans representing calls to Redis adhere to the general [Semantic Conventions for Database Client Spans](/docs/db/database-spans.md).
entities.note:
`db.system.name` MUST be set to `"redis"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

**Span name** SHOULD follow the general [database span name convention](/docs/db/database-spans.md#name)
except that `db.namespace` SHOULD NOT be used in the span name since it is a numeric value that ends up
looking confusing.

entities.name: db.redis.database_index
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `db.namespace` instead.

entities.name: db.response.returned_rows
entities.brief: Number of rows returned by the operation.

entities.name: db.response.status_code
entities.brief: Database response status code.
entities.note:
The status code returned by the database. Usually it represents an error code, but may also represent partial success, warning, or differentiate between various types of successful outcomes.
Semantic conventions for individual database systems SHOULD document what `db.response.status_code` means in the context of that system.

entities.name: db.sql
entities.brief: Attributes for SQL databases

entities.name: db.sql_server.client
entities.brief: Spans representing calls to Microsoft SQL Server adhere to the general [Semantic Conventions for Database Client Spans](database-spans.md).
entities.note: `db.system.name` MUST be set to `"microsoft.sql_server"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

entities.name: db.sql.client
entities.brief: The SQL databases Semantic Conventions describes how common [Database Semantic Conventions](/docs/db/database-spans.md) apply to SQL databases.
entities.note:
The following database systems (defined in the
[`db.system.name`](/docs/db/database-spans.md#notes-and-well-known-identifiers-for-dbsystemname) set)
are known to use SQL as their primary query language:

- `actian.ingres`
- `cockroachdb`
- `derby`
- `firebirdsql`
- `h2database`
- `hsqldb`
- `ibm.db2`
- `mariadb`
- `microsoft.sql_server`
- `mysql`
- `oracle.db`
- `other_sql`
- `postgresql`
- `sap.maxdb`
- `sqlite`
- `trino`

Many other database systems support SQL and can be accessed via generic database driver such as JDBC or ODBC.
Instrumentations applied to generic SQL drivers SHOULD adhere to SQL semantic conventions.

**Span name** SHOULD follow the general [database span name convention](/docs/db/database-spans.md#name)

entities.name: db.sql.table
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `db.collection.name` instead.

deprecation.note: Replaced by `db.collection.name`, but only if not extracting the value from `db.query.text`.

entities.name: db.statement
entities.brief: The database statement being executed.

entities.name: db.stored_procedure.name
entities.brief: The name of a stored procedure within the database.
entities.note:
It is RECOMMENDED to capture the value as provided by the application
without attempting to do any case normalization.

For batch operations, if the individual operations are known to have the same
stored procedure name then that stored procedure name SHOULD be used.

entities.name: db.system
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `db.system.name` instead.

entities.name: db.system.name
entities.brief: The database management system (DBMS) product as identified by the client instrumentation.
entities.note: The actual DBMS may differ from the one identified by the client. For example, when using PostgreSQL client libraries to connect to a CockroachDB, the `db.system.name` is set to `postgresql` based on the instrumentation's best knowledge.

entities.name: db.user
entities.brief: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: deployment
entities.brief: The software deployment.

entities.name: deployment.environment
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `deployment.environment.name` instead.

entities.name: deployment.environment.name
entities.brief: Name of the [deployment environment](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Deployment_environment) (aka deployment tier).
entities.note:
`deployment.environment.name` does not affect the uniqueness constraints defined through
the `service.namespace`, `service.name` and `service.instance.id` resource attributes.
This implies that resources carrying the following attribute combinations MUST be
considered to be identifying the same service:

- `service.name=frontend`, `deployment.environment.name=production`
- `service.name=frontend`, `deployment.environment.name=staging`.

entities.name: deployment.id
entities.brief: The id of the deployment.

entities.name: deployment.name
entities.brief: The name of the deployment.

entities.name: deployment.status
entities.brief: The status of the deployment.

entities.name: destination.address
entities.brief: Destination address - domain name if available without reverse DNS lookup; otherwise, IP address or Unix domain socket name.
entities.note: When observed from the source side, and when communicating through an intermediary, `destination.address` SHOULD represent the destination address behind any intermediaries, for example proxies, if it's available.

entities.name: destination.port
entities.brief: Destination port number

entities.name: device
entities.brief: The device on which the process represented by this resource is running.

entities.name: device.app.lifecycle
entities.brief: This event represents an occurrence of a lifecycle transition on Android or iOS platform.
entities.note:
The event body fields MUST be used to describe the state of the application at the time of the event.
This event is meant to be used in conjunction with `os.name` [resource semantic convention](/docs/resource/os.md) to identify the mobile operating system (e.g. Android, iOS).
The `android.app.state` and `ios.app.state` fields are mutually exclusive and MUST NOT be used together, each field MUST be used with its corresponding `os.name` value.

entities.name: device.id
entities.brief: A unique identifier representing the device
entities.note:
Its value SHOULD be identical for all apps on a device and it SHOULD NOT change if an app is uninstalled and re-installed.
However, it might be resettable by the user for all apps on a device.
Hardware IDs (e.g. vendor-specific serial number, IMEI or MAC address) MAY be used as values.

More information about Android identifier best practices can be found in the [Android user data IDs guide](https://developer.android.com/training/articles/user-data-ids).

> [!WARNING]
>
> This attribute may contain sensitive (PII) information. Caution should be taken when storing personal data or anything which can identify a user. GDPR and data protection laws may apply,
> ensure you do your own due diligence.
>
> Due to these reasons, this identifier is not recommended for consumer applications and will likely result in rejection from both Google Play and App Store.
> However, it may be appropriate for specific enterprise scenarios, such as kiosk devices or enterprise-managed devices, with appropriate compliance clearance.
> Any instrumentation providing this identifier MUST implement it as an opt-in feature.
>
> See [`app.installation.id`](/docs/registry/attributes/app.md#app-installation-id) for a more privacy-preserving alternative.

entities.name: device.manufacturer
entities.brief: The name of the device manufacturer
entities.note: The Android OS provides this field via [Build](https://developer.android.com/reference/android/os/Build#MANUFACTURER). iOS apps SHOULD hardcode the value `Apple`.

entities.name: device.model.identifier
entities.brief: The model identifier for the device
entities.note: It's recommended this value represents a machine-readable version of the model identifier rather than the market or consumer-friendly name of the device.

entities.name: device.model.name
entities.brief: The marketing name for the device model
entities.note: It's recommended this value represents a human-readable version of the device model rather than a machine-readable alternative.

entities.name: disk.io.direction
entities.brief: The disk IO operation direction.

entities.name: dns.answers
entities.brief: The list of IPv4 or IPv6 addresses resolved during DNS lookup.

entities.name: dns.lookup.duration
entities.brief: Measures the time taken to perform a DNS lookup.

entities.name: dns.question.name
entities.brief: The name being queried.
entities.note: The name represents the queried domain name as it appears in the DNS query without any additional normalization.

entities.name: dotnet.assembly.count
entities.brief: The number of .NET assemblies that are currently loaded.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`AppDomain.CurrentDomain.GetAssemblies().Length`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.appdomain.getassemblies).

entities.name: dotnet.dns.lookup.internal
entities.brief: The span describes DNS lookup or reverse lookup performed with one of the methods on [System.Net.Dns](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.net.dns) class.
entities.note:
DNS spans track logical operations rather than physical DNS calls and the actual behavior depends on the
resolver implementation which could be changed in the future versions of .NET.
.NET 9 uses OS DNS resolver which may do zero or more physical lookups for one API call.

When the *DNS lookup* span is reported along with *HTTP connection setup* and *socket connect* span,
the *DNS lookup* span span becomes a child of *HTTP connection setup* and a sibling of *socket connect*.

DNS lookup duration is also reported by [`dns.lookup.duration` metric](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-dns-metrics.md#metric-dnslookupduration).

Corresponding `Activity.OperationName` is `Experimental.System.Net.NameResolution.DnsLookup`, `ActivitySource` name - `Experimental.System.Net.NameResolution`.
Added in .NET 9.

**Span name** SHOULD be `DNS lookup {dns.question.name}` for DNS lookup (IP addresses from host name)
and `DNS reverse lookup {dns.question.name}` for reverse lookup (host names from IP address).

entities.name: dotnet.exceptions
entities.brief: The number of exceptions that have been thrown in managed code.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as counting calls to [`AppDomain.CurrentDomain.FirstChanceException`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.appdomain.firstchanceexception).

entities.name: dotnet.gc.collections
entities.brief: The number of garbage collections that have occurred since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric uses the [`GC.CollectionCount(int generation)`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gc.collectioncount) API to calculate exclusive collections per generation.

entities.name: dotnet.gc.heap.generation
entities.brief: Name of the garbage collector managed heap generation.

entities.name: dotnet.gc.heap.total_allocated
entities.brief: The _approximate_ number of bytes allocated on the managed GC heap since the process has started. The returned value does not include any native allocations.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`GC.GetTotalAllocatedBytes()`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gc.gettotalallocatedbytes).

entities.name: dotnet.gc.last_collection.heap.fragmentation.size
entities.brief: The heap fragmentation, as observed during the latest garbage collection.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`GC.GetGCMemoryInfo().GenerationInfo.FragmentationAfterBytes`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gcgenerationinfo.fragmentationafterbytes).

entities.name: dotnet.gc.last_collection.heap.size
entities.brief: The managed GC heap size (including fragmentation), as observed during the latest garbage collection.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`GC.GetGCMemoryInfo().GenerationInfo.SizeAfterBytes`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gcgenerationinfo.sizeafterbytes).

entities.name: dotnet.gc.last_collection.memory.committed_size
entities.brief: The amount of committed virtual memory in use by the .NET GC, as observed during the latest garbage collection.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`GC.GetGCMemoryInfo().TotalCommittedBytes`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gcmemoryinfo.totalcommittedbytes). Committed virtual memory may be larger than the heap size because it includes both memory for storing existing objects (the heap size) and some extra memory that is ready to handle newly allocated objects in the future.

entities.name: dotnet.gc.pause.time
entities.brief: The total amount of time paused in GC since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`GC.GetTotalPauseDuration()`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.gc.gettotalpauseduration).

entities.name: dotnet.http.connection_setup.internal
entities.brief: The span describes the establishment of the HTTP connection. It includes the time it takes to resolve the DNS, establish the socket connection, and perform the TLS handshake.
entities.note:
There is no parent-child relationship between the [*HTTP client request*](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-client-request) and the
[*HTTP connection setup*]/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md(/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-connection-setup) spans;
the latter will always be a root span, defining a separate trace.

However, if the connection attempt represented by the [*HTTP connection setup*](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-connection-setup) span results in a
successful HTTP connection, and that connection is picked up by a request to serve it, the instrumentation adds a link
to the [*HTTP client request*](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-client-request) span pointing to the *HTTP connection setup* span.
I.e., each request is linked to the connection that served this request.

Corresponding `Activity.OperationName` is `Experimental.System.Net.Http.Connections.ConnectionSetup`, `ActivitySource` name - `Experimental.System.Net.Http.Connections`.
Added in .NET 9.

**Span name** SHOULD be `HTTP connection_setup {server.address}:{server.port}`.

entities.name: dotnet.http.request.wait_for_connection.internal
entities.brief: The span describes the time it takes for the HTTP request to obtain a connection from the connection pool.
entities.note:
The span is reported only if there was no connection readily available when request has started.
It's reported as a child of *HTTP client request* span.

The span ends when the connection is obtained - it could happen when an existing connection becomes available or once
a new connection is established, so the duration of *Wait For Connection* span is different from duration of the
[*HTTP connection setup*](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-connection-setup) span.

The time it takes to get a connection from the pool is also reported by the
[`http.client.request.time_in_queue` metric](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-http-metrics.md#metric-httpclientrequesttime_in_queue).

Corresponding `Activity.OperationName` is `Experimental.System.Net.Http.Connections.WaitForConnection`, `ActivitySource` name - `Experimental.System.Net.Http`.
Added in .NET 9.

**Span name** SHOULD be `HTTP wait_for_connection {server.address}:{server.port}`.

entities.name: dotnet.jit.compilation.time
entities.brief: The amount of time the JIT compiler has spent compiling methods since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`JitInfo.GetCompilationTime()`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.runtime.jitinfo.getcompilationtime).

entities.name: dotnet.jit.compiled_il.size
entities.brief: Count of bytes of intermediate language that have been compiled since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`JitInfo.GetCompiledILBytes()`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.runtime.jitinfo.getcompiledilbytes).

entities.name: dotnet.jit.compiled_methods
entities.brief: The number of times the JIT compiler (re)compiled methods since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`JitInfo.GetCompiledMethodCount()`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.runtime.jitinfo.getcompiledmethodcount).

entities.name: dotnet.monitor.lock_contentions
entities.brief: The number of times there was contention when trying to acquire a monitor lock since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`Monitor.LockContentionCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.threading.monitor.lockcontentioncount).

entities.name: dotnet.process.cpu.count
entities.brief: The number of processors available to the process.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as accessing [`Environment.ProcessorCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.environment.processorcount).

entities.name: dotnet.process.cpu.time
entities.brief: CPU time used by the process.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as accessing the corresponding processor time properties on [`System.Diagnostics.Process`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.diagnostics.process).

entities.name: dotnet.process.memory.working_set
entities.brief: The number of bytes of physical memory mapped to the process context.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`Environment.WorkingSet`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.environment.workingset).

entities.name: dotnet.socket.connect.internal
entities.brief: The span describes the establishment of the socket connection.
entities.note:
It's different from [*HTTP connection setup*](/docs/dotnet/dotnet-network-traces.md#http-connection-setup) span, which also covers the DNS lookup and TLS handshake.

When *socket connect* span is reported along with *HTTP connection setup* span, the socket span becomes a child of HTTP connection setup.

Corresponding `Activity.OperationName` is `Experimental.System.Net.Sockets.Connect`, `ActivitySource` name - `Experimental.System.Net.Sockets`.
Added in .NET 9.

**Span name** SHOULD be `socket connect {network.peer.address}:{network.peer.port}` when socket address family has a
notion of port and `socket connect {network.peer.address}`
otherwise.

entities.name: dotnet.thread_pool.queue.length
entities.brief: The number of work items that are currently queued to be processed by the thread pool.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`ThreadPool.PendingWorkItemCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.threading.threadpool.pendingworkitemcount).

entities.name: dotnet.thread_pool.thread.count
entities.brief: The number of thread pool threads that currently exist.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`ThreadPool.ThreadCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.threading.threadpool.threadcount).

entities.name: dotnet.thread_pool.work_item.count
entities.brief: The number of work items that the thread pool has completed since the process has started.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`ThreadPool.CompletedWorkItemCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.threading.threadpool.completedworkitemcount).

entities.name: dotnet.timer.count
entities.brief: The number of timer instances that are currently active.
entities.note:
Meter name: `System.Runtime`; Added in: .NET 9.0.
This metric reports the same values as calling [`Timer.ActiveCount`](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.threading.timer.activecount).

entities.name: dotnet.tls.handshake.internal
entities.brief: The span describes TLS client or server handshake performed with [System.Net.Security.SslStream](https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.net.security.sslstream).
entities.note:
When *TLS* span is reported for client-side authentication along with *HTTP connection setup* and *socket connect* span, the *TLS* span becomes a child of *HTTP connection setup*.

Corresponding `Activity.OperationName` is `Experimental.System.Net.Security.TlsHandshake`, `ActivitySource` name - `Experimental.System.Net.Security`.
Added in .NET 9.

**Span name** SHOULD be `TLS client handshake {server.address}` when authenticating on the client
side and `TLS server handshake` when authenticating the server.

**Span kind** SHOULD be `INTERNAL` in both cases.

entities.name: elasticsearch.node.name
entities.brief: Represents the human-readable identifier of the node/instance to which a request was routed.

entities.name: enduser.id
entities.brief: Unique identifier of an end user in the system. It maybe a username, email address, or other identifier.
entities.note:
Unique identifier of an end user in the system.

> [!Warning]
> This field contains sensitive (PII) information.

entities.name: enduser.pseudo.id
entities.brief: Pseudonymous identifier of an end user. This identifier should be a random value that is not directly linked or associated with the end user's actual identity.
entities.note:
Pseudonymous identifier of an end user.

> [!Warning]
> This field contains sensitive (linkable PII) information.

entities.name: enduser.role
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `user.roles` instead.

entities.name: enduser.scope
entities.brief: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: error.message
entities.brief: A message providing more detail about an error in human-readable form.
entities.note:
`error.message` should provide additional context and detail about an error.
It is NOT RECOMMENDED to duplicate the value of `error.type` in `error.message`.
It is also NOT RECOMMENDED to duplicate the value of `exception.message` in `error.message`.

`error.message` is NOT RECOMMENDED for metrics or spans due to its unbounded cardinality and overlap with span status.
examples: The user has exceeded their storage quota

deprecation.note: Use domain-specific error message attribute. For example, use `feature_flag.error.message` for feature flag errors.

entities.name: error.type
entities.brief: Describes a class of error the operation ended with.
entities.note:
The `error.type` SHOULD be predictable, and SHOULD have low cardinality.

When `error.type` is set to a type (e.g., an exception type), its
canonical class name identifying the type within the artifact SHOULD be used.

If the recorded error type is a wrapper that is not meaningful for
failure classification, instrumentation MAY use the type of the inner
error instead. For example, in Go, errors created with `fmt.Errorf`
using `%w` MAY be unwrapped when the wrapper type does not help
classify the failure.

Instrumentations SHOULD document the list of errors they report.

The cardinality of `error.type` within one instrumentation library SHOULD be low.
Telemetry consumers that aggregate data from multiple instrumentation libraries and applications
should be prepared for `error.type` to have high cardinality at query time when no
additional filters are applied.

If the operation has completed successfully, instrumentations SHOULD NOT set `error.type`.

If a specific domain defines its own set of error identifiers (such as HTTP or RPC status codes),
it's RECOMMENDED to:

- Use a domain-specific attribute
- Set `error.type` to capture all errors, regardless of whether they are defined within the domain-specific set or not.

entities.name: event.name
entities.brief: Identifies the class / type of event.

deprecation.note: The value of this attribute MUST now be set as the value of the EventName field on the LogRecord to indicate that the LogRecord represents an Event.

entities.name: exception
entities.brief: This event describes a single exception.

entities.name: exception.escaped
entities.brief: Indicates that the exception is escaping the scope of the span.

deprecation.note: It's no longer recommended to record exceptions that are handled and do not escape the scope of a span.

entities.name: exception.message
entities.brief: The exception message.
entities.note:
> [!WARNING]
>
> This attribute may contain sensitive information.
examples: Can't convert 'int' object to str implicitly

entities.name: exception.stacktrace
entities.brief: A stacktrace as a string in the natural representation for the language runtime. The representation is to be determined and documented by each language SIG.
entities.examples:
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.RuntimeException: Test exception
 at com.example.GenerateTrace.methodB(GenerateTrace.java:13)
 at com.example.GenerateTrace.methodA(GenerateTrace.java:9)
 at com.example.GenerateTrace.main(GenerateTrace.java:5)

entities.name: exception.type
entities.brief: The type of the exception (its fully-qualified class name, if applicable). The dynamic type of the exception should be preferred over the static type in languages that support it.
entities.note:
If the recorded exception type is a wrapper that is not meaningful for
failure classification, instrumentation MAY use the type of the inner
exception instead. For example, in Go, errors created with `fmt.Errorf`
using `%w` MAY be unwrapped when the wrapper type does not help
classify the failure.

entities.name: faas
entities.brief: A serverless instance.

entities.name: faas
entities.brief: This semantic convention describes an instance of a function that runs without provisioning or managing of servers (also known as serverless functions or Function as a Service (FaaS)) with spans.

entities.name: faas_resource
entities.brief: A serverless instance.

entities.name: faas_span
entities.brief: This semantic convention describes an instance of a function that runs without provisioning or managing of servers (also known as serverless functions or Function as a Service (FaaS)) with spans.

entities.name: faas_span.datasource
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS triggered as a response to some data source operation such as a database or filesystem read/write.

entities.name: faas_span.http
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS triggered as a response to some data source operation such as a database or filesystem read/write.

entities.name: faas_span.in
entities.brief: Contains additional attributes for incoming FaaS spans.

entities.name: faas_span.out
entities.brief: Contains additional attributes for outgoing FaaS spans.

entities.name: faas_span.pubsub
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS set to be executed when messages are sent to a messaging system.

entities.name: faas_span.timer
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS scheduled to be executed regularly.

entities.name: faas.client
entities.brief: This span represents an outgoing call to a FaaS service.
entities.note:
The values reported by the client for the attributes listed below SHOULD be equal to
the corresponding [FaaS resource attributes][] and [Cloud resource attributes][],
which the invoked FaaS instance reports about itself, if it's instrumented.

entities.name: faas.coldstart
entities.brief: A boolean that is true if the serverless function is executed for the first time (aka cold-start).

entities.name: faas.coldstarts
entities.brief: Number of invocation cold starts.

entities.name: faas.cpu_usage
entities.brief: Distribution of CPU usage per invocation.

entities.name: faas.cron
entities.brief: A string containing the schedule period as [Cron Expression](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E12058_01/doc/doc.1014/e12030/cron_expressions.htm).

entities.name: faas.datasource
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS triggered as a response to some data source operation such as a database or filesystem read/write.

entities.name: faas.datasource.server
entities.brief: This span represents server side if the FaaS invocations triggered in response response to some data source operation such as a database or filesystem read/write.

entities.name: faas.document.collection
entities.brief: The name of the source on which the triggering operation was performed. For example, in Cloud Storage or S3 corresponds to the bucket name, and in Cosmos DB to the database name.

entities.name: faas.document.name
entities.brief: The document name/table subjected to the operation. For example, in Cloud Storage or S3 is the name of the file, and in Cosmos DB the table name.

entities.name: faas.document.operation
entities.brief: Describes the type of the operation that was performed on the data.

entities.name: faas.document.time
entities.brief: A string containing the time when the data was accessed in the [ISO 8601](https://www.iso.org/iso-8601-date-and-time-format.html) format expressed in [UTC](https://www.w3.org/TR/NOTE-datetime).

entities.name: faas.errors
entities.brief: Number of invocation errors.

entities.name: faas.init_duration
entities.brief: Measures the duration of the function's initialization, such as a cold start.

entities.name: faas.instance
entities.brief: The execution environment ID as a string, that will be potentially reused for other invocations to the same function/function version.
entities.note: - **AWS Lambda:** Use the (full) log stream name.

entities.name: faas.invocation_id
entities.brief: The invocation ID of the current function invocation.

entities.name: faas.invocation.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during FaaS function invocation, such as application errors, internal failures, or other exceptions that prevent the function from completing successfully.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during FaaS function invocation.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to ERROR (severity number 17) when recording this event.
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding FaaS span.

entities.name: faas.invocations
entities.brief: Number of successful invocations.

entities.name: faas.invoke_duration
entities.brief: Measures the duration of the function's logic execution.

entities.name: faas.invoked_name
entities.brief: The name of the invoked function.
entities.note: SHOULD be equal to the `faas.name` resource attribute of the invoked function.

entities.name: faas.invoked_provider
entities.brief: The cloud provider of the invoked function.
entities.note: SHOULD be equal to the `cloud.provider` resource attribute of the invoked function.

entities.name: faas.invoked_region
entities.brief: The cloud region of the invoked function.
entities.note: SHOULD be equal to the `cloud.region` resource attribute of the invoked function.

entities.name: faas.max_memory
entities.brief: The amount of memory available to the serverless function converted to Bytes.
entities.note: It's recommended to set this attribute since e.g. too little memory can easily stop a Java AWS Lambda function from working correctly. On AWS Lambda, the environment variable `AWS_LAMBDA_FUNCTION_MEMORY_SIZE` provides this information (which must be multiplied by 1,048,576).

entities.name: faas.mem_usage
entities.brief: Distribution of max memory usage per invocation.

entities.name: faas.name
entities.brief: The name of the single function that this runtime instance executes.
entities.note:
This is the name of the function as configured/deployed on the FaaS
platform and is usually different from the name of the callback
function (which may be stored in the
[`code.namespace`/`code.function.name`](/docs/general/attributes.md#source-code-attributes)
span attributes).

For some cloud providers, the above definition is ambiguous. The following
definition of function name MUST be used for this attribute
(and consequently the span name) for the listed cloud providers/products:

- **Azure:**  The full name `<FUNCAPP>/<FUNC>`, i.e., function app name
  followed by a forward slash followed by the function name (this form
  can also be seen in the resource JSON for the function).
  This means that a span attribute MUST be used, as an Azure function
  app can host multiple functions that would usually share
  a TracerProvider (see also the `cloud.resource_id` attribute).

entities.name: faas.net_io
entities.brief: Distribution of net I/O usage per invocation.

entities.name: faas.server
entities.brief: This span represents server (incoming) side of the FaaS invocation.

entities.name: faas.time
entities.brief: A string containing the function invocation time in the [ISO 8601](https://www.iso.org/iso-8601-date-and-time-format.html) format expressed in [UTC](https://www.w3.org/TR/NOTE-datetime).

entities.name: faas.timeouts
entities.brief: Number of invocation timeouts.

entities.name: faas.timer
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for FaaS scheduled to be executed regularly.

entities.name: faas.timer.server
entities.brief: This span represents server side if the FaaS invocations triggered by a timer.

entities.name: faas.trigger
entities.brief: Type of the trigger which caused this function invocation.

entities.name: faas.version
entities.brief: The immutable version of the function being executed.
entities.note:
Depending on the cloud provider and platform, use:

- **AWS Lambda:** The [function version](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-versions.html)
  (an integer represented as a decimal string).
- **Google Cloud Run (Services):** The [revision](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/managing/revisions)
  (i.e., the function name plus the revision suffix).
- **Google Cloud Functions:** The value of the
  [`K_REVISION` environment variable](https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/container-contract#services-env-vars).
- **Azure Functions:** Not applicable. Do not set this attribute.

entities.name: feature_flag
entities.brief: This semantic convention defines the attributes used to represent a feature flag evaluation as an event.

entities.name: feature_flag.context.id
entities.brief: The unique identifier for the flag evaluation context. For example, the targeting key.

entities.name: feature_flag.error.message
entities.brief: A message providing more detail about an error that occurred during feature flag evaluation in human-readable form.
examples: The user has exceeded their storage quota

entities.name: feature_flag.evaluation
entities.brief: Defines feature flag evaluation as an event.
entities.note: A `feature_flag.evaluation` event SHOULD be emitted whenever a feature flag value is evaluated, which may happen many times over the course of an application lifecycle. For example, a website A/B testing different animations may evaluate a flag each time a button is clicked. A `feature_flag.evaluation` event is emitted on each evaluation even if the result is the same.

entities.name: feature_flag.evaluation.error.message
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `feature_flag.error.message` instead.
examples: Flag `header-color` expected type `string` but found type `number`

entities.name: feature_flag.evaluation.reason
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `feature_flag.result.reason` instead.

entities.name: feature_flag.key
entities.brief: The lookup key of the feature flag.

entities.name: feature_flag.provider_name
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `feature_flag.provider.name` instead.

entities.name: feature_flag.provider.name
entities.brief: Identifies the feature flag provider.

entities.name: feature_flag.result.reason
entities.brief: The reason code which shows how a feature flag value was determined.

entities.name: feature_flag.result.value
entities.brief: The evaluated value of the feature flag.
entities.note:
With some feature flag providers, feature flag results can be quite large or contain private or sensitive details.
Because of this, `feature_flag.result.variant` is often the preferred attribute if it is available.

It may be desirable to redact or otherwise limit the size and scope of `feature_flag.result.value` if possible.
Because the evaluated flag value is unstructured and may be any type, it is left to the instrumentation author to determine how best to achieve this.

entities.name: feature_flag.result.variant
entities.brief: A semantic identifier for an evaluated flag value.
entities.note:
A semantic identifier, commonly referred to as a variant, provides a means
for referring to a value without including the value itself. This can
provide additional context for understanding the meaning behind a value.
For example, the variant `red` maybe be used for the value `#c05543`.

entities.name: feature_flag.set.id
entities.brief: The identifier of the [flag set](https://openfeature.dev/specification/glossary/#flag-set) to which the feature flag belongs.

entities.name: feature_flag.variant
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `feature_flag.result.variant` instead.

entities.name: feature_flag.version
entities.brief: The version of the ruleset used during the evaluation. This may be any stable value which uniquely identifies the ruleset.

entities.name: file.accessed
entities.brief: Time when the file was last accessed, in ISO 8601 format.
entities.note: This attribute might not be supported by some file systems — NFS, FAT32, in embedded OS, etc.

entities.name: file.attributes
entities.brief: Array of file attributes.
entities.note: Attributes names depend on the OS or file system. Here’s a non-exhaustive list of values expected for this attribute: `archive`, `compressed`, `directory`, `encrypted`, `execute`, `hidden`, `immutable`, `journaled`, `read`, `readonly`, `symbolic link`, `system`, `temporary`, `write`.

entities.name: file.changed
entities.brief: Time when the file attributes or metadata was last changed, in ISO 8601 format.
entities.note: `file.changed` captures the time when any of the file's properties or attributes (including the content) are changed, while `file.modified` captures the timestamp when the file content is modified.

entities.name: file.created
entities.brief: Time when the file was created, in ISO 8601 format.
entities.note: This attribute might not be supported by some file systems — NFS, FAT32, in embedded OS, etc.

entities.name: file.directory
entities.brief: Directory where the file is located. It should include the drive letter, when appropriate.

entities.name: file.extension
entities.brief: File extension, excluding the leading dot.
entities.note: When the file name has multiple extensions (example.tar.gz), only the last one should be captured ("gz", not "tar.gz").

entities.name: file.fork_name
entities.brief: Name of the fork. A fork is additional data associated with a filesystem object.
entities.note:
On Linux, a resource fork is used to store additional data with a filesystem object. A file always has at least one fork for the data portion, and additional forks may exist.
On NTFS, this is analogous to an Alternate Data Stream (ADS), and the default data stream for a file is just called $DATA. Zone.Identifier is commonly used by Windows to track contents downloaded from the Internet. An ADS is typically of the form: C:\path\to\filename.extension:some_fork_name, and some_fork_name is the value that should populate `fork_name`. `filename.extension` should populate `file.name`, and `extension` should populate `file.extension`. The full path, `file.path`, will include the fork name.

entities.name: file.group.id
entities.brief: Primary Group ID (GID) of the file.

entities.name: file.group.name
entities.brief: Primary group name of the file.

entities.name: file.inode
entities.brief: Inode representing the file in the filesystem.

entities.name: file.lock.mechanism
entities.brief: The lock mechanism such as noted by [POSIX](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/functions/fcntl.html)

entities.name: file.lock.mode
entities.brief: Mode of lock or operation such as documented by [POSIX](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/functions/fcntl.html)

entities.name: file.lock.type
entities.brief: The lock type as represented by i.e. [POSIX](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/functions/fcntl.html)'s l_type.

entities.name: file.mode
entities.brief: Mode of the file in octal representation.

entities.name: file.modified
entities.brief: Time when the file content was last modified, in ISO 8601 format.

entities.name: file.name
entities.brief: Name of the file including the extension, without the directory.

entities.name: file.owner.id
entities.brief: The user ID (UID) or security identifier (SID) of the file owner.

entities.name: file.owner.name
entities.brief: Username of the file owner.

entities.name: file.path
entities.brief: Full path to the file, including the file name. It should include the drive letter, when appropriate.

entities.name: file.size
entities.brief: File size in bytes.

entities.name: file.symbolic_link.target_path
entities.brief: Path to the target of a symbolic link.
entities.note: This attribute is only applicable to symbolic links.

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.application.container
entities.brief: The container within GCP where the AppHub destination application is defined.

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.application.id
entities.brief: The name of the destination application as configured in AppHub.

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.application.location
entities.brief: The GCP zone or region where the destination application is defined.

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.service.criticality_type
entities.brief: Criticality of a destination workload indicates its importance to the business as specified in [AppHub type enum](https://cloud.google.com/app-hub/docs/reference/rest/v1/Attributes#type)

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.service.environment_type
entities.brief: Software lifecycle stage of a destination service as defined [AppHub environment type](https://cloud.google.com/app-hub/docs/reference/rest/v1/Attributes#type_1)

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.service.id
entities.brief: The name of the destination service as configured in AppHub.

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.workload.criticality_type
entities.brief: Criticality of a destination workload indicates its importance to the business as specified in [AppHub type enum](https://cloud.google.com/app-hub/docs/reference/rest/v1/Attributes#type)

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.workload.environment_type
entities.brief: Environment of a destination workload is the stage of a software lifecycle as provided in the [AppHub environment type](https://cloud.google.com/app-hub/docs/reference/rest/v1/Attributes#type_1)

entities.name: gcp.apphub_destination.workload.id
entities.brief: The name of the destination workload as configured in AppHub.

entities.name: gcp.apphub.application
entities.brief: Attributes denoting data from an Application in AppHub. See [AppHub overview](https://cloud.google.com/app-hub/docs/overview).

entities.name: gcp.apphub.application.container
entities.brief: The container within GCP where the AppHub application is defined.

entities.name: gcp.client.service

entities.note: Intended to be a stable identifier for Google Cloud client libraries that is uniform across implementation languages. The value should be derived from the canonical service domain for the service; for example, 'foo.googleapis.com' should result in a value of 'foo'.

entities.name: gcp.cloud_run
entities.brief: Resource used by Google Cloud Run.

entities.name: gcp.cloud_run.job.execution

entities.name: gcp.cloud_run.job.task_index

entities.name: gcp.gce
entities.brief: Resources used by Google Compute Engine (GCE).

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance_group_manager
entities.brief: A GCE instance group manager.

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance_group_manager.name
entities.brief: The name of the Instance Group Manager (IGM) that manages this VM, if any.

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance_group_manager.region
entities.brief: The region of a **regional** Instance Group Manager (e.g., `us-central1`). Set this **only** when the IGM is regional.

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance_group_manager.zone
entities.brief: The zone of a **zonal** Instance Group Manager (e.g., `us-central1-a`). Set this **only** when the IGM is zonal.

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance.hostname
entities.brief: The hostname of a GCE instance. This is the full value of the default or [custom hostname](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/custom-hostname-vm).

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance.labels
entities.brief: GCE instance labels, `<key>` being the label name and the value being the label value.
entities.note: For example, a GCE instance label `team` with value `observability` SHOULD be recorded as the `gcp.gce.instance.labels.team` attribute with value `"observability"`. The `<key>` MUST be the exact GCE instance label key.

entities.name: gcp.gce.instance.name
entities.brief: The instance name of a GCE instance. This is the value provided by `host.name`, the visible name of the instance in the Cloud Console UI, and the prefix for the default hostname of the instance as defined by the [default internal DNS name](https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/internal-dns#instance-fully-qualified-domain-names).

entities.note:

entities.note: This event is opt-in and could be used to store input and output details independently from traces.

entities.name: gen_ai.client.operation.duration

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.client.operation.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during a Generative AI client operation, such as API errors, rate limiting, model errors, timeouts, or other errors that prevent the operation from completing successfully.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during Generative AI client operations.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to WARN (severity number 13) when recording this event.
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding Generative AI client span.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.client.operation.time_per_output_chunk
entities.brief: Time per output chunk, recorded for each chunk received after the first one, measured as the time elapsed from the end of the previous chunk to the end of the current chunk.
entities.note: This metrics SHOULD be reported for streaming calls and SHOULD NOT be reported otherwise.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.client.operation.time_to_first_chunk
entities.brief: Time to receive the first chunk, measured from when the client issues the generation request to when the first chunk is received in the response stream.
entities.note: This metrics SHOULD be reported for streaming calls and SHOULD NOT be reported otherwise.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.brief: In the lifetime of an GenAI span, events for prompts sent and completions received may be created, depending on the configuration of the instrumentation.

entities.name: gen_ai.content.prompt
entities.brief: In the lifetime of an GenAI span, events for prompts sent and completions received may be created, depending on the configuration of the instrumentation.

entities.name: gen_ai.conversation.id
entities.brief: The unique identifier for a conversation (session, thread), used to store and correlate messages within this conversation.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.create_agent.client
entities.brief: Describes GenAI agent creation and is usually applicable when working with remote agent services.
entities.note:
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `create_agent`.

**Span name** SHOULD be `create_agent {gen_ai.agent.name}`.
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.data_source.id
entities.brief: The data source identifier.
entities.note: Data sources are used by AI agents and RAG applications to store grounding data. A data source may be an external database, object store, document collection, website, or any other storage system used by the GenAI agent or application. The `gen_ai.data_source.id` SHOULD match the identifier used by the GenAI system rather than a name specific to the external storage, such as a database or object store. Semantic conventions referencing `gen_ai.data_source.id` MAY also leverage additional attributes, such as `db.*`, to further identify and describe the data source.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.embeddings.client
entities.brief:
Describes GenAI embeddings span - a request to a Generative AI model or service that generates an embeddings based on the input.
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `embeddings`.
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}`.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.embeddings.dimension.count
entities.brief: The number of dimensions the resulting output embeddings should have.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.evaluation.explanation
entities.brief: A free-form explanation for the assigned score provided by the evaluator.
examples: The response is factually accurate but lacks sufficient detail to fully address the question.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.evaluation.name
entities.brief: The name of the evaluation metric used for the GenAI response.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.evaluation.result
entities.brief: This event captures the result of evaluating GenAI output for quality, accuracy, or other characteristics. This event SHOULD be parented to GenAI operation span being evaluated when possible or set `gen_ai.response.id` when span id is not available.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.evaluation.score.label
entities.brief: Human readable label for evaluation.
entities.note: This attribute provides a human-readable interpretation of the evaluation score produced by an evaluator. For example, a score value of 1 could mean "relevant" in one evaluation system and "not relevant" in another, depending on the scoring range and evaluator. The label SHOULD have low cardinality. Possible values depend on the evaluation metric and evaluator used; implementations SHOULD document the possible values.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.evaluation.score.value
entities.brief: The evaluation score returned by the evaluator.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.execute_tool.internal
entities.brief: Describes tool execution span.
entities.note:
`gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `execute_tool`.

**Span name** SHOULD be `execute_tool {gen_ai.tool.name}`.

GenAI instrumentations that can instrument tool execution calls SHOULD do so,
unless another instrumentation can reliably cover all supported tool types.
MCP tool executions may also be traced by the
[corresponding MCP instrumentation](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/mcp.md#client).

Tools are often executed directly by application code. Application developers
are encouraged to follow this semantic convention for tools invoked by their
own code and to manually instrument any tool calls that automatic
instrumentations do not cover.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.inference.client
entities.brief: This span represents a client call to Generative AI model or service that generates a response or requests a tool call based on the input prompt.
entities.note:
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}`.
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format
and MUST follow the overall [guidelines for span names](https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-specification/blob/v1.58.0/specification/trace/api.md#span).

**Span kind** SHOULD be `CLIENT` and MAY be set to `INTERNAL` on spans representing
call to models running in the same process. It's RECOMMENDED to use `CLIENT` kind
when the GenAI system being instrumented usually runs in a different process than its
client or when the GenAI call happens over instrumented protocol such as HTTP.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.input.messages
entities.brief: The chat history provided to the model as an input.
entities.note:
Instrumentations MUST follow [Input messages JSON schema](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-input-messages.json).
When the attribute is recorded on events, it MUST be recorded in structured
form. When recorded on spans, it MAY be recorded as a JSON string if structured
format is not supported and SHOULD be recorded in structured form otherwise.

Messages MUST be provided in the order they were sent to the model.
Instrumentations MAY provide a way for users to filter or truncate
input messages.

> [!Warning]
> This attribute is likely to contain sensitive information including user/PII data.

See [Recording content on attributes](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-spans.md#recording-content-on-attributes)
section for more details.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.invoke_agent.client
entities.brief: Describes GenAI agent invocation over a remote service.
entities.note:
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `invoke_agent`.

**Span name** SHOULD be `invoke_agent {gen_ai.agent.name}` if `gen_ai.agent.name` is readily available.
When `gen_ai.agent.name` is not available, it SHOULD be `invoke_agent`.
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format.

Examples: OpenAI Assistants API, AWS Bedrock Agents.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.invoke_agent.internal
entities.brief: Describes GenAI agent invocation within the same process.
entities.note:
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `invoke_agent`.

**Span name** SHOULD be `invoke_agent {gen_ai.agent.name}` if `gen_ai.agent.name` is readily available.
When `gen_ai.agent.name` is not available, it SHOULD be `invoke_agent`.
Semantic conventions for individual GenAI systems and frameworks MAY specify different span name format.

Examples: LangChain agents, CrewAI agents.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.invoke_workflow.internal
entities.brief: Represents an operation that executes a coordinated process composed of multiple agents or other operations involving generative AI.
entities.note:
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `invoke_workflow`.
**Span name** SHOULD be `invoke_workflow {gen_ai.workflow.name}`.

This span SHOULD be reported by the instrumentations when they can
reliably determine that invocation is a workflow (i.e. groups several agent
invocations) and SHOULD NOT be reported by instrumentations that
can't distinguish it `invoke_workflow` from `invoke_agent`.
eg: Some frameworks like ADK have workflow agents that orchestrate other agents
and report `invoke_agent` spans, so `invoke_workflow` SHOULD NOT be reported by such instrumentations.
Conversely, frameworks like CrewAI have a distinct concept of crew (similar to workflow)
that is separate from individual agents, so they SHOULD report `invoke_workflow` spans.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.openai.client
entities.brief: Describes an OpenAI operation span.

entities.name: gen_ai.openai.inference.client

entities.note:
`gen_ai.system` MUST be set to `"openai"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}`.

entities.brief: Deprecated, use `openai.response.system_fingerprint`.

deprecation.note: Replaced by `openai.response.system_fingerprint`, which has moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.operation.name
entities.brief: The name of the operation being performed.
entities.note: If one of the predefined values applies, but specific system uses a different name it's RECOMMENDED to document it in the semantic conventions for specific GenAI system and use system-specific name in the instrumentation. If a different name is not documented, instrumentation libraries SHOULD use applicable predefined value.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.output.messages
entities.brief: Messages returned by the model where each message represents a specific model response (choice, candidate).
entities.note:
Instrumentations MUST follow [Output messages JSON schema](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-output-messages.json)

Each message represents a single output choice/candidate generated by
the model. Each message corresponds to exactly one generation
(choice/candidate) and vice versa - one choice cannot be split across
multiple messages or one message cannot contain parts from multiple choices.

When the attribute is recorded on events, it MUST be recorded in structured
form. When recorded on spans, it MAY be recorded as a JSON string if structured
format is not supported and SHOULD be recorded in structured form otherwise.

Instrumentations MAY provide a way for users to filter or truncate
output messages.

> [!Warning]
> This attribute is likely to contain sensitive information including user/PII data.

See [Recording content on attributes](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-spans.md#recording-content-on-attributes)
section for more details.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.output.type
entities.brief: Represents the content type requested by the client.
entities.note:
This attribute SHOULD be used when the client requests output of a specific type. The model may return zero or more outputs of this type.
This attribute specifies the output modality and not the actual output format. For example, if an image is requested, the actual output could be a URL pointing to an image file.
Additional output format details may be recorded in the future in the `gen_ai.output.{type}.*` attributes.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.prompt
entities.brief: Deprecated, use Event API to report prompt contents.

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: gen_ai.prompt.name
entities.brief: The name of the prompt that uniquely identifies it.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.provider.name
entities.brief: The Generative AI provider as identified by the client or server instrumentation.
entities.note:
The attribute SHOULD be set based on the instrumentation's best
knowledge and may differ from the actual model provider.

Multiple providers, including Azure OpenAI, Gemini, and AI hosting platforms
are accessible using the OpenAI REST API and corresponding client libraries,
but may proxy or host models from different providers.

The `gen_ai.request.model`, `gen_ai.response.model`, and `server.address`
attributes may help identify the actual system in use.

The `gen_ai.provider.name` attribute acts as a discriminator that
identifies the GenAI telemetry format flavor specific to that provider
within GenAI semantic conventions.
It SHOULD be set consistently with provider-specific attributes and signals.
For example, GenAI spans, metrics, and events related to AWS Bedrock
should have the `gen_ai.provider.name` set to `aws.bedrock` and include
applicable `aws.bedrock.*` attributes and are not expected to include
`openai.*` attributes.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.request
entities.brief: A request to an LLM is modeled as a span in a trace. The span name should be a low cardinality value representing the request made to an LLM, like the name of the API endpoint being called.

entities.name: gen_ai.request.choice.count

entities.note: In some GenAI systems the encoding formats are called embedding types. Also, some GenAI systems only accept a single format per request.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: gen_ai.retrieval.client

Describes GenAI retrieval span - a request to a Generative AI service or framework that retrieves relevant information or context from a vector database or search system.
The `gen_ai.operation.name` SHOULD be `retrieval`.
**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.data_source.id}`. Semantic conventions for individual GenAI providers and retrievers MAY specify different span name format.

entities.name: gen_ai.retrieval.documents
entities.brief: The documents retrieved.
entities.note:
Instrumentations MUST follow [Retrieval documents JSON schema](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-retrieval-documents.json).
When the attribute is recorded on events, it MUST be recorded in structured
form. When recorded on spans, it MAY be recorded as a JSON string if structured
format is not supported and SHOULD be recorded in structured form otherwise.

Each document object SHOULD contain at least the following properties:
`id` (string): A unique identifier for the document, `score` (double): The relevance score of the document

entities.name: gen_ai.system_instructions
entities.brief: The system message or instructions provided to the GenAI model separately from the chat history.
entities.note:
This attribute SHOULD be used when the corresponding provider or API
allows to provide system instructions or messages separately from the
chat history.

Instructions that are part of the chat history SHOULD be recorded in
`gen_ai.input.messages` attribute instead.

Instrumentations MUST follow [System instructions JSON schema](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-system-instructions.json).

When recorded on spans, it MAY be recorded as a JSON string if structured
format is not supported and SHOULD be recorded in structured form otherwise.

Instrumentations MAY provide a way for users to filter or truncate
system instructions.

> [!Warning]
> This attribute may contain sensitive information.

See [Recording content on attributes](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-spans.md#recording-content-on-attributes)
section for more details.

entities.name: gen_ai.tool.type
entities.brief: Type of the tool utilized by the agent
entities.note:
Extension: A tool executed on the agent-side to directly call external APIs, bridging the gap between the agent and real-world systems.
  Agent-side operations involve actions that are performed by the agent on the server or within the agent's controlled environment.
Function: A tool executed on the client-side, where the agent generates parameters for a predefined function, and the client executes the logic.
  Client-side operations are actions taken on the user's end or within the client application.
Datastore: A tool used by the agent to access and query structured or unstructured external data for retrieval-augmented tasks or knowledge updates.

entities.name: go.cpu.state
entities.brief: The state of the CPU.

entities.name: go.cpu.time
entities.brief: Estimated CPU time spent by the Go runtime.
entities.note: Computed from `/cpu/classes/...` metrics. This metric is an overestimate, and not directly comparable to system CPU time measurements. Compare only with other `go.cpu.time` metrics.

entities.name: go.goroutine.count
entities.brief: Count of live goroutines.
entities.note: Computed from `/sched/goroutines:goroutines`.

entities.name: go.memory.allocated
entities.brief: Memory allocated to the heap by the application.
entities.note: Computed from `/gc/heap/allocs:bytes`.

entities.name: go.memory.allocations
entities.brief: Count of allocations to the heap by the application.
entities.note: Computed from `/gc/heap/allocs:objects`.

entities.name: go.memory.detailed_type
entities.brief: The detailed type of memory.
entities.note: Value SHOULD match the specific memory class reported by the Go runtime under `/memory/classes/...`. The list of possible values is subject to change with the Go version used.

entities.name: go.memory.gc.cycles
entities.brief: Number of completed GC cycles.
entities.note: Computed from `/gc/cycles/total:gc-cycles`.

entities.name: go.memory.gc.goal
entities.brief: Heap size target for the end of the GC cycle.
entities.note: Computed from `/gc/heap/goal:bytes`.

entities.name: go.memory.gc.pause.duration
entities.brief: Distribution of individual GC-related stop-the-world pause latencies. This is the time from deciding to stop the world until the world is started again.
entities.note: Computed from `/sched/pauses/total/gc:seconds`. Bucket boundaries are provided by the runtime, and are subject to change.

entities.name: go.memory.limit
entities.brief: Go runtime memory limit configured by the user, if a limit exists.
entities.note: Computed from `/gc/gomemlimit:bytes`. This metric is excluded if the limit obtained from the Go runtime is math.MaxInt64.

entities.name: go.memory.type
entities.brief: The type of memory.

entities.name: go.memory.used
entities.brief: Memory used by the Go runtime.
entities.note: Computed from `(/memory/classes/total:bytes - /memory/classes/heap/released:bytes)`.

entities.name: go.processor.limit
entities.brief: The number of OS threads that can execute user-level Go code simultaneously.
entities.note: Computed from `/sched/gomaxprocs:threads`.

entities.name: go.schedule.duration
entities.brief: The time goroutines have spent in the scheduler in a runnable state before actually running.
entities.note: Computed from `/sched/latencies:seconds`. Bucket boundaries are provided by the runtime, and are subject to change.

entities.name: graphql
entities.brief: This document defines semantic conventions to apply when instrumenting the GraphQL implementation. They map GraphQL operations to attributes on a Span.

entities.name: graphql.document
entities.brief: The GraphQL document being executed.
entities.note: If instrumentation can reliably identify and redact sensitive information it SHOULD do it.

entities.name: graphql.operation.name
entities.brief: The name of the operation being executed.

entities.name: graphql.operation.type
entities.brief: The type of the operation being executed.

entities.name: graphql.server
entities.brief: This span represents an incoming operation on a GraphQL server implementation.
entities.note:
**Span name** SHOULD be of the format `{graphql.operation.type}` provided
`graphql.operation.type` is available. If `graphql.operation.type` is not available,
the span SHOULD be named `GraphQL Operation`.

> [!WARNING]
> The `graphql.operation.name` value is provided by the client and can have high
> cardinality. Using it in the GraphQL server span name (by default) is
> NOT RECOMMENDED.
>
> Instrumentation MAY provide a configuration option to enable a more descriptive
> span name following `{graphql.operation.type} {graphql.operation.name}` format
> when `graphql.operation.name` is available.

entities.name: host

entities.name: host.arch

entities.name: host.cpu
entities.brief: A host's CPU information

entities.name: host.cpu.cache.l2.size
entities.brief: The amount of level 2 memory cache available to the processor (in Bytes).

entities.name: host.cpu.family
entities.brief: Family or generation of the CPU.

entities.name: host.cpu.model.id
entities.brief: Model identifier. It provides more granular information about the CPU, distinguishing it from other CPUs within the same family.

entities.name: host.cpu.model.name

entities.name: host.cpu.stepping
