entities.brief: Processor manufacturer identifier. A maximum 12-character string.
entities.note: [CPUID](https://wiki.osdev.org/CPUID) command returns the vendor ID string in EBX, EDX and ECX registers. Writing these to memory in this order results in a 12-character string.

entities.name: host.id
entities.brief: Unique host ID. For Cloud, this must be the instance_id assigned by the cloud provider. For non-containerized systems, this should be the `machine-id`. See the table below for the sources to use to determine the `machine-id` based on operating system.

entities.name: host.image.id
entities.brief: VM image ID or host OS image ID. For Cloud, this value is from the provider.

entities.name: host.image.name
entities.brief: Name of the VM image or OS install the host was instantiated from.

entities.name: host.image.version
entities.brief: The version string of the VM image or host OS as defined in [Version Attributes](/docs/resource/README.md#version-attributes).

entities.name: host.ip
entities.brief: Available IP addresses of the host, excluding loopback interfaces.
entities.note: IPv4 Addresses MUST be specified in dotted-quad notation. IPv6 addresses MUST be specified in the [RFC 5952](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5952.html) format.

entities.name: host.mac
entities.brief: Available MAC addresses of the host, excluding loopback interfaces.
entities.note: MAC Addresses MUST be represented in [IEEE RA hexadecimal form](https://standards.ieee.org/wp-content/uploads/import/documents/tutorials/eui.pdf): as hyphen-separated octets in uppercase hexadecimal form from most to least significant.

entities.name: host.name
entities.brief: Name of the host. On Unix systems, it may contain what the hostname command returns, or the fully qualified hostname, or another name specified by the user.

entities.name: host.type
entities.brief: Type of host. For Cloud, this must be the machine type.

entities.name: http.client
entities.brief: This span represents an outbound HTTP request.
entities.note:
There are two ways HTTP client spans can be implemented in an instrumentation:

1. Instrumentations SHOULD create an HTTP span for each attempt to send an HTTP request over the wire.
   In case the request is resent, the resend attempts MUST follow the [HTTP resend spec](#http-request-retries-and-redirects).
   In this case, instrumentations SHOULD NOT (also) emit a logical encompassing HTTP client span.

2. If for some reason it is not possible to emit a span for each send attempt (because e.g. the instrumented library does not expose hooks that would allow this),
   instrumentations MAY create an HTTP span for the top-most operation of the HTTP client.
   In this case, the `url.full` MUST be the absolute URL that was originally requested, before any HTTP-redirects that may happen when executing the request.

**Span name:** refer to the [Span Name](/docs/http/http-spans.md#name) section.

**Span kind** MUST be `CLIENT`.

**Span status:** refer to the [Span Status](/docs/http/http-spans.md#status) section.

entities.name: http.client_ip
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `client.address` instead.

entities.name: http.client.active_requests
entities.brief: Number of active HTTP requests.

entities.name: http.client.connection.duration
entities.brief: The duration of the successfully established outbound HTTP connections.

entities.name: http.client.open_connections
entities.brief: Number of outbound HTTP connections that are currently active or idle on the client.

entities.name: http.client.request.body.size
entities.brief: Size of HTTP client request bodies.
entities.note: The size of the request payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.client.request.duration
entities.brief: Duration of HTTP client requests.

entities.name: http.client.request.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during an HTTP client request, such as network failures, timeouts, or other errors that prevent the request from completing successfully.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during HTTP client operations.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to WARN (severity number 13) when recording this event.
Some HTTP client frameworks generate artificial exceptions for non-successful HTTP status codes (e.g., 404 Not Found). When possible, instrumentations SHOULD NOT record these artificial exceptions, or SHOULD set the severity to DEBUG (severity number 5).
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding HTTP client span.

entities.name: http.client.response.body.size
entities.brief: Size of HTTP client response bodies.
entities.note: The size of the response payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.connection.state
entities.brief: State of the HTTP connection in the HTTP connection pool.

entities.name: http.flavor
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `network.protocol.name` and `network.protocol.version` instead.

entities.name: http.host
entities.brief: Deprecated, use one of `server.address`, `client.address` or `http.request.header.host` instead, depending on the usage.

deprecation.note: Replaced by one of `server.address`, `client.address` or `http.request.header.host`, depending on the usage.

entities.name: http.method
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.request.method` instead.

entities.name: http.request_content_length
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.request.header.content-length` instead.

entities.name: http.request_content_length_uncompressed
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.request.body.size` instead.

entities.name: http.request.body.size
entities.brief: The size of the request payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.request.header
entities.brief: HTTP request headers, `<key>` being the normalized HTTP Header name (lowercase), the value being the header values.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD require an explicit configuration of which headers are to be captured.
Including all request headers can be a security risk - explicit configuration helps avoid leaking sensitive information.

The `User-Agent` header is already captured in the `user_agent.original` attribute.
Users MAY explicitly configure instrumentations to capture them even though it is not recommended.

The attribute value MUST consist of either multiple header values as an array of strings
or a single-item array containing a possibly comma-concatenated string, depending on the way
the HTTP library provides access to headers.

Examples:

- A header `Content-Type: application/json` SHOULD be recorded as the `http.request.header.content-type`
  attribute with value `["application/json"]`.
- A header `X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4, 1.2.3.5` SHOULD be recorded as the `http.request.header.x-forwarded-for`
  attribute with value `["1.2.3.4", "1.2.3.5"]` or `["1.2.3.4, 1.2.3.5"]` depending on the HTTP library.

entities.name: http.request.method
entities.brief: HTTP request method.
entities.note:
HTTP request method value SHOULD be "known" to the instrumentation.
By default, this convention defines "known" methods as the ones listed in [RFC9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#name-methods),
the PATCH method defined in [RFC5789](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc5789.html)
and the QUERY method defined in [httpbis-safe-method-w-body](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-httpbis-safe-method-w-body/?include_text=1).

If the HTTP request method is not known to instrumentation, it MUST set the `http.request.method` attribute to `_OTHER`.

If the HTTP instrumentation could end up converting valid HTTP request methods to `_OTHER`, then it MUST provide a way to override
the list of known HTTP methods. If this override is done via environment variable, then the environment variable MUST be named
OTEL_INSTRUMENTATION_HTTP_KNOWN_METHODS and support a comma-separated list of case-sensitive known HTTP methods.

![Development](https://img.shields.io/badge/-development-blue)
If this override is done via declarative configuration, then the list MUST be configurable via the `known_methods` property
(an array of case-sensitive strings with minimum items 0) under `.instrumentation/development.general.http.client` and/or
`.instrumentation/development.general.http.server`.

In either case, this list MUST be a full override of the default known methods,
it is not a list of known methods in addition to the defaults.

HTTP method names are case-sensitive and `http.request.method` attribute value MUST match a known HTTP method name exactly.
Instrumentations for specific web frameworks that consider HTTP methods to be case insensitive, SHOULD populate a canonical equivalent.
Tracing instrumentations that do so, MUST also set `http.request.method_original` to the original value.

entities.name: http.request.method_original
entities.brief: Original HTTP method sent by the client in the request line.

entities.name: http.request.resend_count
entities.brief: The ordinal number of request resending attempt (for any reason, including redirects).
entities.note: The resend count SHOULD be updated each time an HTTP request gets resent by the client, regardless of what was the cause of the resending (e.g. redirection, authorization failure, 503 Server Unavailable, network issues, or any other).

entities.name: http.request.size
entities.brief: The total size of the request in bytes. This should be the total number of bytes sent over the wire, including the request line (HTTP/1.1), framing (HTTP/2 and HTTP/3), headers, and request body if any.

entities.name: http.response_content_length
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.response.header.content-length` instead.

entities.name: http.response_content_length_uncompressed
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.response.body.size` instead.

entities.name: http.response.body.size
entities.brief: The size of the response payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.response.header
entities.brief: HTTP response headers, `<key>` being the normalized HTTP Header name (lowercase), the value being the header values.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD require an explicit configuration of which headers are to be captured.
Including all response headers can be a security risk - explicit configuration helps avoid leaking sensitive information.

Users MAY explicitly configure instrumentations to capture them even though it is not recommended.

The attribute value MUST consist of either multiple header values as an array of strings
or a single-item array containing a possibly comma-concatenated string, depending on the way
the HTTP library provides access to headers.

Examples:

- A header `Content-Type: application/json` header SHOULD be recorded as the `http.request.response.content-type`
  attribute with value `["application/json"]`.
- A header `My-custom-header: abc, def` header SHOULD be recorded as the `http.response.header.my-custom-header`
  attribute with value `["abc", "def"]` or `["abc, def"]` depending on the HTTP library.

entities.name: http.response.size
entities.brief: The total size of the response in bytes. This should be the total number of bytes sent over the wire, including the status line (HTTP/1.1), framing (HTTP/2 and HTTP/3), headers, and response body and trailers if any.

entities.name: http.response.status_code
entities.brief: [HTTP response status code](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-6).

entities.name: http.route
entities.brief: The matched route template for the request. This MUST be low-cardinality and include all static path segments, with dynamic path segments represented with placeholders.
entities.note:
MUST NOT be populated when this is not supported by the HTTP server framework as the route attribute should have low-cardinality and the URI path can NOT substitute it.
SHOULD include the [application root](/docs/http/http-spans.md#http-server-definitions) if there is one.

A static path segment is a part of the route template with a fixed, low-cardinality value. This includes literal strings like `/users/` and placeholders that
are constrained to a finite, predefined set of values, e.g. `{controller}` or `{action}`.

A dynamic path segment is a placeholder for a value that can have high cardinality and is not constrained to a predefined list like static path segments.

Instrumentations SHOULD use routing information provided by the corresponding web framework. They SHOULD pick the most precise source of routing information and MAY
support custom route formatting. Instrumentations SHOULD document the format and the API used to obtain the route string.

entities.name: http.scheme
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `url.scheme` instead.

entities.name: http.server
entities.brief: This span represents an inbound HTTP request.
entities.note:
**Span name:** refer to the [Span Name](/docs/http/http-spans.md#name) section.

**Span kind** MUST be `SERVER`.

**Span status:** refer to the [Span Status](/docs/http/http-spans.md#status) section.

entities.name: http.server_name
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `server.address` instead.

entities.name: http.server.active_requests
entities.brief: Number of active HTTP server requests.

entities.name: http.server.request.body.size
entities.brief: Size of HTTP server request bodies.
entities.note: The size of the request payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.server.request.duration
entities.brief: Duration of HTTP server requests.

entities.name: http.server.request.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during HTTP server request processing, such as application errors, internal failures, or other exceptions that prevent the server from successfully handling the request.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during HTTP server request processing.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to ERROR (severity number 17) when recording this event.
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding HTTP server span.

entities.name: http.server.response.body.size
entities.brief: Size of HTTP server response bodies.
entities.note: The size of the response payload body in bytes. This is the number of bytes transferred excluding headers and is often, but not always, present as the [Content-Length](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.content-length) header. For requests using transport encoding, this should be the compressed size.

entities.name: http.status_code
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `http.response.status_code` instead.

entities.name: http.target
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `url.path` and `url.query` instead.

entities.name: http.url
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `url.full` instead.

entities.name: http.user_agent
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `user_agent.original` instead.
examples: Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 14_7_1 like Mac OS X) AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/14.1.2 Mobile/15E148 Safari/604.1

entities.note: `hw.status` is currently specified as an *UpDownCounter* but would ideally be represented using a [*StateSet* as defined in OpenMetrics](https://github.com/prometheus/OpenMetrics/blob/v1.0.0/specification/OpenMetrics.md#stateset). This semantic convention will be updated once *StateSet* is specified in OpenTelemetry. This planned change is not expected to have any consequence on the way users query their timeseries backend to retrieve the values of `hw.status` over time.

entities.name: ios.state

entities.note:
Under the [JSON-RPC specification](https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification), the `id` property may be a string, number, null, or omitted entirely. When omitted, the request is treated as a notification. Using `null` is not equivalent to omitting the `id`, but it is discouraged.
Instrumentations SHOULD NOT capture this attribute when the `id` is `null` or omitted.

entities.name: k8s.cluster
entities.brief: A Kubernetes Cluster.

entities.name: k8s.cluster.name
entities.brief: The name of the cluster.

entities.name: k8s.cluster.uid
entities.brief: A pseudo-ID for the cluster, set to the UID of the `kube-system` namespace.
entities.note:
K8s doesn't have support for obtaining a cluster ID. If this is ever
added, we will recommend collecting the `k8s.cluster.uid` through the
official APIs. In the meantime, we are able to use the `uid` of the
`kube-system` namespace as a proxy for cluster ID. Read on for the
rationale.

Every object created in a K8s cluster is assigned a distinct UID. The
`kube-system` namespace is used by Kubernetes itself and will exist
for the lifetime of the cluster. Using the `uid` of the `kube-system`
namespace is a reasonable proxy for the K8s ClusterID as it will only
change if the cluster is rebuilt. Furthermore, Kubernetes UIDs are
UUIDs as standardized by
[ISO/IEC 9834-8 and ITU-T X.667](https://www.itu.int/ITU-T/studygroups/com17/oid.html).
Which states:

> If generated according to one of the mechanisms defined in Rec.
> ITU-T X.667 | ISO/IEC 9834-8, a UUID is either guaranteed to be
> different from all other UUIDs generated before 3603 A.D., or is
> extremely likely to be different (depending on the mechanism chosen).

Therefore, UIDs between clusters should be extremely unlikely to
conflict.

entities.name: k8s.container
entities.brief: A container in a [PodTemplate](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/workloads/pods/#pod-templates).

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.limit
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `k8s.container.cpu.limit.desired` and `k8s.container.cpu.limit.current` instead.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.limit_utilization
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `k8s.container.cpu.limit.utilization` instead.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.limit.current
entities.brief: Maximum CPU resource limit currently configured for a running container.
entities.note:
This metric aligns with the limit in the
[`resources`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#resourcerequirements-v1-core) field of
[K8s ContainerStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#containerstatus-v1-core)
(status.containerStatuses[*].resources). Also see `Actual Resources` in
<https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/resize-container-resources/> for more details.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.limit.desired
entities.brief: Maximum CPU resource limit as defined by the container spec.
entities.note:
This metric aligns with the limit in the
[`resources`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#resourcerequirements-v1-core) field of
[K8s Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#container-v1-core)
(spec.containers[*].resources). Also see `Desired Resources` in
<https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/resize-container-resources/> for more details.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.limit.utilization
entities.brief: The ratio of container CPU usage to its current CPU limit.
entities.note:
The current CPU limit reflects the actual resources applied to the container, as reported by
[ContainerStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#containerstatus-v1-core).
The value range is [0.0,1.0]. A value of 1.0 means the container is using 100% of its actual CPU limit.
If the CPU limit is not set, this metric SHOULD NOT be emitted for that container.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.request
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `k8s.container.cpu.request.desired` and `k8s.container.cpu.request.current` instead.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.request_utilization
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `k8s.container.cpu.request.utilization` instead.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.request.current
entities.brief: CPU resource requested currently configured for a running container.
entities.note:
This metric aligns with the request in the
[`resources`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#resourcerequirements-v1-core) field of
[K8s ContainerStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#containerstatus-v1-core)

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.request.desired
entities.brief: CPU resource requested as defined by the container spec.
entities.note:
This metric aligns with the request in the
[`resources`](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#resourcerequirements-v1-core) field of
[K8s Container](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#container-v1-core)

<https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/configure-pod-container/resize-container-resources/> for more details.

entities.name: k8s.container.cpu.request.utilization
entities.brief: The ratio of container CPU usage to its current CPU request.
entities.note:
The current CPU request reflects the request applied to the running container, as reported by
[ContainerStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#containerstatus-v1-core).
The value range is [0.0,1.0]. A value of 1.0 means the container is using 100% of its actual CPU request.
If the CPU request is not set, this metric SHOULD NOT be emitted for that container.

entities.name: k8s.container.ephemeral_storage.fs_type

entities.note: Eviction decisions based on ephemeral-storage resource limits are made based on the total container usage.

entities.name: k8s.container.name
entities.brief: The name of the Container from Pod specification, must be unique within a Pod. Container runtime usually uses different globally unique name (`container.name`).

entities.name: k8s.container.ready
entities.brief: Indicates whether the container is currently marked as ready to accept traffic, based on its readiness probe (1 = ready, 0 = not ready).
entities.note:
This metric SHOULD reflect the value of the `ready` field in the
[K8s ContainerStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#containerstatus-v1-core).

entities.name: k8s.container.restart_count
entities.brief: Number of times the container was restarted. This attribute can be used to identify a particular container (running or stopped) within a container spec.

entities.name: k8s.container.restart.count
entities.brief: Describes how many times the container has restarted (since the last counter reset).
entities.note:
This value is pulled directly from the K8s API and the value can go indefinitely high and be reset to 0
at any time depending on how your kubelet is configured to prune dead containers.
It is best to not depend too much on the exact value but rather look at it as
either == 0, in which case you can conclude there were no restarts in the recent past, or > 0, in which case
you can conclude there were restarts in the recent past, and not try and analyze the value beyond that.

entities.name: k8s.container.status.last_terminated_reason

entities.name: k8s.container.status.reason

entities.note:
All possible container state reasons will be reported at each time interval to avoid missing metrics.
Only the value corresponding to the current state reason will be non-zero.

Only the value corresponding to the current state will be non-zero.

entities.name: k8s.namespace

entities.name: k8s.namespace.annotation
entities.brief: The annotation placed on the Namespace, the `<key>` being the annotation name, the value being the annotation value, even if the value is empty.
entities.note:
Examples:

- An annotation `ttl` with value `0` SHOULD be recorded
  as the `k8s.namespace.annotation.ttl` attribute with value `"0"`.
- An annotation `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.namespace.annotation.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.namespace.label

entities.note:

- A label `kubernetes.io/metadata.name` with value `default` SHOULD be recorded
  as the `k8s.namespace.label.kubernetes.io/metadata.name` attribute with value `"default"`.
- A label `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.namespace.label.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.namespace.phase

entities.name: k8s.namespace.phase

entities.name: k8s.node.annotation

entities.note:

- An annotation `node.alpha.kubernetes.io/ttl` with value `0` SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.node.annotation.node.alpha.kubernetes.io/ttl` attribute with value `"0"`.
- An annotation `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as

entities.name: k8s.node.condition.status
entities.brief: The status of the condition, one of True, False, Unknown.
entities.note:
This attribute aligns with the `status` field of the
[NodeCondition](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#nodecondition-v1-core)

entities.name: k8s.node.condition.status
entities.brief: Describes the condition of a particular Node.
entities.note: All possible node condition pairs (type and status) will be reported at each time interval to avoid missing metrics. Condition pairs corresponding to the current conditions' statuses will be non-zero.

entities.name: k8s.node.condition.type
entities.brief: The condition type of a K8s Node.
entities.note:
K8s Node conditions as described
by [K8s documentation](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/node/node-status/#condition).

This attribute aligns with the `type` field of the
[NodeCondition](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#nodecondition-v1-core)

The set of possible values is not limited to those listed here. Managed Kubernetes environments,
or custom controllers MAY introduce additional node condition types.
When this occurs, the exact value as reported by the Kubernetes API SHOULD be used.

entities.name: k8s.node.cpu.allocatable
entities.brief: Amount of cpu allocatable on the node.

entities.name: k8s.node.cpu.time
entities.brief: Total CPU time consumed.
entities.note: Total CPU time consumed by the specific Node on all available CPU cores

entities.name: k8s.node.cpu.usage
entities.brief: Node's CPU usage, measured in cpus. Range from 0 to the number of allocatable CPUs.
entities.note: CPU usage of the specific Node on all available CPU cores, averaged over the sample window

entities.name: k8s.node.ephemeral_storage.allocatable
entities.brief: Amount of ephemeral-storage allocatable on the node.

entities.name: k8s.node.filesystem.available
entities.brief: Node filesystem available bytes.

This metric is derived from the
[FsStats.AvailableBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#FsStats) field
of the [NodeStats.Fs](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats)
of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.filesystem.capacity

This metric is derived from the
[FsStats.CapacityBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#FsStats) field
of the [NodeStats.Fs](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats)
of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.filesystem.usage
entities.brief: Node filesystem usage.
entities.note:
This may not equal capacity - available.

This metric is derived from the
[FsStats.UsedBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#FsStats) field
of the [NodeStats.Fs](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats)

entities.name: k8s.node.label

- A label `kubernetes.io/arch` with value `arm64` SHOULD be recorded
  as the `k8s.node.label.kubernetes.io/arch` attribute with value `"arm64"`.
- A label `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.node.label.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.allocatable
entities.brief: Amount of memory allocatable on the node.

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.available
entities.brief: Node memory available.
entities.note:
Available memory for use.  This is defined as the memory limit - workingSetBytes. If memory limit is undefined, the available bytes is omitted.
This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.AvailableBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) field of the [NodeStats.Memory](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.paging.faults
entities.brief: Node memory paging faults.
entities.note:
Cumulative number of major/minor page faults.
This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.PageFaults](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) and [MemoryStats.MajorPageFaults](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) fields of the [NodeStats.Memory](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.rss
entities.brief: Node memory RSS.
entities.note:
The amount of anonymous and swap cache memory (includes transparent hugepages).
This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.RSSBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) field of the [NodeStats.Memory](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.usage
entities.brief: Memory usage of the Node.
entities.note: Total memory usage of the Node

entities.name: k8s.node.memory.working_set
entities.brief: Node memory working set.
entities.note:
The amount of working set memory. This includes recently accessed memory, dirty memory, and kernel memory. WorkingSetBytes is <= UsageBytes.
This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.WorkingSetBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) field of the [NodeStats.Memory](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#NodeStats) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.name
entities.brief: The name of the Node.

entities.name: k8s.node.network.errors
entities.brief: Node network errors.

entities.name: k8s.node.network.io
entities.brief: Network bytes for the Node.

entities.name: k8s.node.pod.allocatable
entities.brief: Amount of pods allocatable on the node.

entities.name: k8s.node.system_container

entities.name: k8s.node.system_container.cpu.time

entities.name: k8s.node.system_container.memory.usage

entities.note: This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.UsageBytes](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L252) field of the [ContainerStats](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L157C6-L157C20) of [Node.SystemContainers](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L40) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.system_container.memory.working_set
entities.brief: The amount of working set memory.
entities.note: This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.WorkingSetBytes](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L256) field of the [ContainerStats](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L157C6-L157C20) of [Node.SystemContainers](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubelet/blob/v0.35.2/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1/types.go#L40) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.node.system_container.name
entities.brief: The name of the system container running on the K8s Node.

entities.name: k8s.node.uid
entities.brief: The UID of the Node.

entities.name: k8s.node.uptime
entities.brief: The time the Node has been running.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD use a gauge with type `double` and measure uptime in seconds as a floating point number with the highest precision available.
The actual accuracy would depend on the instrumentation and operating system.

entities.name: k8s.persistentvolumeclaim.name
entities.brief: The name of the PersistentVolumeClaim.

entities.name: k8s.persistentvolumeclaim.status.phase

All possible phases should be reported at each interval to avoid gaps in the time series.

entities.note:
The amount of working set memory. This includes recently accessed memory, dirty memory, and kernel memory. WorkingSetBytes is <= UsageBytes.
This metric is derived from the [MemoryStats.WorkingSetBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#MemoryStats) field of the [PodStats.Memory](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.34.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.pod.name
entities.brief: The name of the Pod.

entities.name: k8s.pod.network.errors
entities.brief: Pod network errors.

entities.name: k8s.pod.network.io
entities.brief: Network bytes for the Pod.

entities.name: k8s.pod.start_time
entities.brief: The start timestamp of the Pod.
entities.note:
Date and time at which the object was acknowledged by the Kubelet.
This is before the Kubelet pulled the container image(s) for the pod.

This attribute aligns with the `startTime` field of the
[K8s PodStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.34/#podstatus-v1-core),
in ISO 8601 (RFC 3339 compatible) format.

entities.name: k8s.pod.status.phase
entities.brief: The phase for the pod. Corresponds to the `phase` field of the: [K8s PodStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.33/#podstatus-v1-core)

entities.name: k8s.pod.status.phase
entities.brief: Describes number of K8s Pods that are currently in a given phase.
entities.note:
All possible pod phases will be reported at each time interval to avoid missing metrics.
Only the value corresponding to the current phase will be non-zero.

entities.name: k8s.pod.status.reason
entities.brief: The reason for the pod state. Corresponds to the `reason` field of the: [K8s PodStatus](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/generated/kubernetes-api/v1.33/#podstatus-v1-core)

entities.name: k8s.pod.status.reason
entities.brief: Describes the number of K8s Pods that are currently in a state for a given reason.
entities.note:
All possible pod status reasons will be reported at each time interval to avoid missing metrics.
Only the value corresponding to the current reason will be non-zero.

entities.name: k8s.pod.uid
entities.brief: The UID of the Pod.

entities.name: k8s.pod.uptime
entities.brief: The time the Pod has been running.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD use a gauge with type `double` and measure uptime in seconds as a floating point number with the highest precision available.
The actual accuracy would depend on the instrumentation and operating system.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.available
entities.brief: Pod volume storage space available.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.AvailableBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.capacity
entities.brief: Pod volume total capacity.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.CapacityBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.inode.count
entities.brief: The total inodes in the filesystem of the Pod's volume.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.Inodes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.inode.free
entities.brief: The free inodes in the filesystem of the Pod's volume.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.InodesFree](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.inode.used
entities.brief: The inodes used by the filesystem of the Pod's volume.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.InodesUsed](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

This may not be equal to `inodes - free` because filesystem may share inodes with other filesystems.

entities.name: k8s.pod.volume.usage
entities.brief: Pod volume usage.
entities.note:
This may not equal capacity - available.

This metric is derived from the
[VolumeStats.UsedBytes](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#VolumeStats) field
of the [PodStats](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/kubelet@v0.33.0/pkg/apis/stats/v1alpha1#PodStats) of the
Kubelet's stats API.

entities.name: k8s.resourcequota.uid

entities.name: k8s.service
entities.brief: A Kubernetes Service object.

entities.name: k8s.service.annotation
entities.brief: The annotation placed on the Service, the `<key>` being the annotation name, the value being the annotation value, even if the value is empty.
entities.note:
Examples:

- An annotation `prometheus.io/scrape` with value `true` SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.annotation.prometheus.io/scrape` attribute with value `"true"`.
- An annotation `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.annotation.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.service.endpoint.address_type
entities.brief: The address type of the service endpoint.
entities.note:
The network address family or type of the endpoint.
This attribute aligns with the `addressType` field of the
[K8s EndpointSlice](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/endpoint-slice-v1/).
It is used to differentiate metrics when a Service is backed by multiple address types
(e.g., in dual-stack clusters).

entities.name: k8s.service.endpoint.condition
entities.brief: The condition of the service endpoint.
entities.note:
The current operational condition of the service endpoint.
An endpoint can have multiple conditions set at once (e.g., both `serving` and `terminating` during rollout).
This attribute aligns with the condition fields in the [K8s EndpointSlice](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/endpoint-slice-v1/).

entities.name: k8s.service.endpoint.count
entities.brief: Number of endpoints for a service by condition and address type.
entities.note:
This metric is derived from the Kubernetes [EndpointSlice API](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/endpoint-slice-v1/).
It reports the number of network endpoints backing a Service, broken down by their condition and address type.

In dual-stack or multi-protocol clusters, separate counts are reported for each address family (`IPv4`, `IPv6`, `FQDN`).

When the optional `zone` attribute is enabled, counts are further broken down by availability zone for zone-aware monitoring.

An endpoint may be reported under multiple conditions simultaneously (e.g., both `serving` and `terminating` during a graceful shutdown).
See [K8s EndpointConditions](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/endpoint-slice-v1/) for more details.

The conditions represent:
- `ready`: Endpoints capable of receiving new connections.
- `serving`: Endpoints currently handling traffic.
- `terminating`: Endpoints that are being phased out but may still be handling existing connections.

For Services with `publishNotReadyAddresses` enabled (common for headless StatefulSets),
this metric will include endpoints that are published despite not being ready.
The `k8s.service.publish_not_ready_addresses` resource attribute indicates this setting.

entities.name: k8s.service.endpoint.zone
entities.brief: The zone of the service endpoint.
entities.note:
The zone where the endpoint is located, typically corresponding to a failure domain.
This attribute aligns with the `zone` field of endpoints in the
[K8s EndpointSlice](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/endpoint-slice-v1/).
It enables zone-aware monitoring of service endpoint distribution and supports
features like [Topology Aware Routing](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/services-networking/topology-aware-routing/).

If the zone is not populated (e.g., nodes without the `topology.kubernetes.io/zone` label),
the attribute value will be an empty string.

entities.name: k8s.service.label
entities.brief: The label placed on the Service, the `<key>` being the label name, the value being the label value, even if the value is empty.
entities.note:
Examples:

- A label `app` with value `my-service` SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.label.app` attribute with value `"my-service"`.
- A label `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.label.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.service.load_balancer.ingress.count
entities.brief: Number of load balancer ingress points (external IPs/hostnames) assigned to the service.
entities.note:
This metric reports the number of external ingress points (IP addresses or hostnames)
assigned to a LoadBalancer Service.

It is only emitted for Services of type `LoadBalancer` and reflects the assignments
made by the underlying infrastructure's load balancer controller in the
[.status.loadBalancer.ingress](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/service-v1/#ServiceStatus) field.

A value of `0` indicates that no ingress points have been assigned yet (e.g., during provisioning).
A value greater than `1` may occur when multiple IPs or hostnames are assigned (e.g., dual-stack configurations).

This metric signals that external endpoints have been assigned by the load balancer controller, but it does not
guarantee that the load balancer is healthy.

entities.name: k8s.service.name
entities.brief: The name of the Service.

entities.name: k8s.service.publish_not_ready_addresses
entities.brief: Whether the Service publishes not-ready endpoints.
entities.note:
Whether the Service is configured to publish endpoints before the pods are ready.
This attribute is typically used to indicate that a Service (such as a headless
Service for a StatefulSet) allows peer discovery before pods pass their readiness probes.
It aligns with the `publishNotReadyAddresses` field of the
[K8s ServiceSpec](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/service-v1/#ServiceSpec).

entities.name: k8s.service.selector
entities.brief: The selector key-value pair placed on the Service, the `<key>` being the selector key, the value being the selector value.
entities.note:
These selectors are used to correlate with pod labels. Each selector key-value pair becomes a separate attribute.

Examples:

- A selector `app=my-app` SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.selector.app` attribute with value `"my-app"`.
- A selector `version=v1` SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.service.selector.version` attribute with value `"v1"`.

entities.name: k8s.service.traffic_distribution
entities.brief: The traffic distribution policy for the Service.
entities.note:
Specifies how traffic is distributed to endpoints for this Service.
This attribute aligns with the `trafficDistribution` field of the
[K8s ServiceSpec](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/networking/virtual-ips/#traffic-distribution).
Known values include `PreferSameZone` (prefer endpoints in the same zone as the client) and
`PreferSameNode` (prefer endpoints on the same node, fallback to same zone, then cluster-wide).
If this field is not set on the Service, the attribute SHOULD NOT be emitted.
When not set, Kubernetes distributes traffic evenly across all endpoints cluster-wide.

entities.name: k8s.service.type
entities.brief: The type of the Kubernetes Service.
entities.note:
This attribute aligns with the `type` field of the
[K8s ServiceSpec](https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/kubernetes-api/service-resources/service-v1/#ServiceSpec).

entities.name: k8s.service.uid
entities.brief: The UID of the Service.

entities.name: k8s.statefulset
entities.brief: A Kubernetes StatefulSet object.

entities.name: k8s.statefulset.annotation
entities.brief: The annotation placed on the StatefulSet, the `<key>` being the annotation name, the value being the annotation value, even if the value is empty.
entities.note:
Examples:

- An annotation `replicas` with value `1` SHOULD be recorded
  as the `k8s.statefulset.annotation.replicas` attribute with value `"1"`.
- An annotation `data` with empty string value SHOULD be recorded as
  the `k8s.statefulset.annotation.data` attribute with value `""`.

entities.name: k8s.statefulset.current_pods
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `k8s.statefulset.pod.current` instead.
entities.note:
This metric aligns with the `currentReplicas` field of the

entities.name: mcp.client
entities.brief: This span describes the MCP call from the client side.
entities.note:
It's reported by the MCP client when it initiates the request
or notification or by the MCP server when server initiates the operation.
It covers the time to receive the response or ack from the peer.

**Span name** SHOULD follow the format `{mcp.method.name} {target}`
where target SHOULD match `{gen_ai.tool.name}` or `{gen_ai.prompt.name}` when
applicable.
If there is no low-cardinality `target` available, the Span name SHOULD be `{mcp.method.name}`.

Instrumentation MAY allow users to opt into including `{mcp.resource.uri}`
as `target` in the span name when it is available but SHOULD NOT include it by default
to avoid high cardinality span names.

**Span status** SHOULD be set to `ERROR` when `error.type` attribute is present.
The status description SHOULD match the `JSONRPCError.message` if the message is available.

Refer to the [Recording Errors](/docs/general/recording-errors.md) document
for more details.

MCP tool call execution spans are compatible with GenAI [execute_tool spans](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/blob/v1.41.0/docs/gen-ai/gen-ai-spans.md#execute-tool-span).

If the MCP instrumentation can reliably detect that outer GenAI instrumentation
is already tracing the tool execution, it SHOULD NOT create a separate span.
Instead, it SHOULD add MCP-specific attributes to the existing tool execution span.

Instrumentations that support this behavior MAY provide a configuration
option to enable it.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.client.operation.duration
entities.brief: The duration of the MCP request or notification as observed on the sender from the time it was sent until the response or ack is received.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.client.session.duration

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.method.name
entities.brief: The name of the request or notification method.

entities.name: mcp.protocol.version
entities.brief: The [version](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/versioning) of the Model Context Protocol used.

entities.name: mcp.resource.uri
entities.brief: The value of the resource uri.
entities.note: This is a URI of the resource provided in the following requests or notifications: `resources/read`, `resources/subscribe`, `resources/unsubscribe`, or `notifications/resources/updated`.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.server
entities.brief: This span describes the processing of the MCP request or notification initiated by the peer.
entities.note:
It's reported by the MCP server when client initiates the request
(or notification) or by the MCP client when server initiates the operation.

**Span name** SHOULD follow the format `{mcp.method.name} {target}`
where target SHOULD match `{gen_ai.tool.name}` or `{gen_ai.prompt.name}` when
applicable.
If there is no low-cardinality `target` available, the Span name SHOULD be `{mcp.method.name}`.

Instrumentation MAY allow users to opt into including `{mcp.resource.uri}`
as `target` in the span name when it is available but SHOULD NOT include it by default
to avoid high cardinality span names.

**Span status** SHOULD be set to `ERROR` when `error.type` attribute is present.
The status description SHOULD match the `JSONRPCError.message` if the message is available.

Refer to the [Recording Errors](/docs/general/recording-errors.md) document
for more details.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.server.operation.duration
entities.brief: MCP request or notification duration as observed on the receiver from the time it was received until the result or ack is sent.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: mcp.server.session.duration
entities.brief: The duration of the MCP session as observed on the MCP server.

entities.name: messaging
entities.brief: Defines a full set of attributes used in messaging systems.

entities.name: messaging.batch.message_count
entities.brief: The number of messages sent, received, or processed in the scope of the batching operation.
entities.note: Instrumentations SHOULD NOT set `messaging.batch.message_count` on spans that operate with a single message. When a messaging client library supports both batch and single-message API for the same operation, instrumentations SHOULD use `messaging.batch.message_count` for batching APIs and SHOULD NOT use it for single-message APIs.

entities.name: messaging.client_id
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `messaging.client.id` instead.

entities.name: messaging.client.consumed.messages
entities.brief: Number of messages that were delivered to the application.
entities.note:
Records the number of messages pulled from the broker or number of messages dispatched to the application in push-based scenarios.
The metric SHOULD be reported once per message delivery. For example, if receiving and processing operations are both instrumented for a single message delivery, this counter is incremented when the message is received and not reported when it is processed.

entities.name: messaging.client.id
entities.brief: A unique identifier for the client that consumes or produces a message.

entities.name: messaging.client.operation.duration
entities.brief: Duration of messaging operation initiated by a producer or consumer client.
entities.note: This metric SHOULD NOT be used to report processing duration - processing duration is reported in `messaging.process.duration` metric.

entities.name: messaging.client.published.messages
entities.brief: Deprecated. Use `messaging.client.sent.messages` instead.

entities.name: messaging.client.sent.messages
entities.brief: Number of messages producer attempted to send to the broker.
entities.note: This metric MUST NOT count messages that were created but haven't yet been sent.

entities.name: messaging.consumer.group.name
entities.brief: The name of the consumer group with which a consumer is associated.
entities.note: Semantic conventions for individual messaging systems SHOULD document whether `messaging.consumer.group.name` is applicable and what it means in the context of that system.

entities.name: messaging.create.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during a messaging create operation.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during a messaging create operation.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to WARN (severity number 13) when recording this event.
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding messaging create span.

entities.name: messaging.destination.anonymous

entities.name: messaging.destination.name
entities.brief: The message destination name
entities.note:
Destination name SHOULD uniquely identify a specific queue, topic or other entity within the broker. If
the broker doesn't have such notion, the destination name SHOULD uniquely identify the broker.

entities.name: messaging.destination.partition.id
entities.brief: The identifier of the partition messages are sent to or received from, unique within the `messaging.destination.name`.

entities.name: messaging.destination.subscription.name

entities.note: Semantic conventions for individual messaging systems SHOULD document whether `messaging.destination.subscription.name` is applicable and what it means in the context of that system.

entities.name: messaging.destination.template
entities.brief: Low cardinality representation of the messaging destination name
entities.note: Destination names could be constructed from templates. An example would be a destination name involving a user name or product id. Although the destination name in this case is of high cardinality, the underlying template is of low cardinality and can be effectively used for grouping and aggregation.

entities.name: messaging.destination.temporary

entities.brief: The network interface name.

entities.name: network.io.direction
entities.brief: The direction of traffic from the perspective of the observing host's physical or virtual network interface. It should not be used to represent the logical direction of a stateful connection or network flow.

entities.name: network.local.address
entities.brief: Local address of the network connection - IP address or Unix domain socket name.

entities.name: network.local.port
entities.brief: Local port number of the network connection.

entities.name: network.peer.address
entities.brief: Peer address of the network connection - IP address or Unix domain socket name.

entities.name: network.peer.port
entities.brief: Peer port number of the network connection.

entities.name: network.protocol.name
entities.brief: [OSI application layer](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Application_layer) or non-OSI equivalent.
entities.note: The value SHOULD be normalized to lowercase.

entities.name: network.protocol.version
entities.brief: The actual version of the protocol used for network communication.
entities.note: If protocol version is subject to negotiation (for example using [ALPN](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7301.html)), this attribute SHOULD be set to the negotiated version. If the actual protocol version is not known, this attribute SHOULD NOT be set.

entities.name: network.transport
entities.brief: [OSI transport layer](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Transport_layer) or [inter-process communication method](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Inter-process_communication).
entities.note:
The value SHOULD be normalized to lowercase.

Consider always setting the transport when setting a port number, since
a port number is ambiguous without knowing the transport. For example
different processes could be listening on TCP port 12345 and UDP port 12345.

entities.name: network.type
entities.brief: [OSI network layer](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Network_layer) or non-OSI equivalent.
entities.note: The value SHOULD be normalized to lowercase.

entities.name: nfs.client.net.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client TCP segments and UDP datagrams handled.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.netudpcnt and svc_stat.nettcpcnt

entities.name: nfs.client.net.tcp.connection.accepted
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client TCP connections accepted.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.nettcpconn

entities.name: nfs.client.operation.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFSv4+ client operations.

entities.name: nfs.client.procedure.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client procedures.

entities.name: nfs.client.rpc.authrefresh.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client RPC authentication refreshes.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.rpcauthrefresh

entities.name: nfs.client.rpc.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client RPCs sent, regardless of whether they're accepted/rejected by the server.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.rpccnt

entities.name: nfs.client.rpc.retransmit.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS client RPC retransmits.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.rpcretrans

entities.name: nfs.operation.name
entities.brief: NFSv4+ operation name.

entities.name: nfs.server.fh.stale.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server stale file handles.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel NFSD_STATS_FH_STALE counter in the nfsd_net struct

entities.name: nfs.server.io
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server bytes returned to receive and transmit (read and write) requests.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel NFSD_STATS_IO_READ and NFSD_STATS_IO_WRITE counters in the nfsd_net struct

entities.name: nfs.server.net.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server TCP segments and UDP datagrams handled.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.nettcpcnt and svc_stat.netudpcnt

entities.name: nfs.server.net.tcp.connection.accepted
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server TCP connections accepted.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.nettcpconn

entities.name: nfs.server.operation.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFSv4+ server operations.

entities.name: nfs.server.procedure.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server procedures.

entities.name: nfs.server.repcache.requests
entities.brief: Reports the kernel NFS server reply cache request count by cache hit status.

entities.name: nfs.server.repcache.status
entities.brief: Linux: one of "hit" (NFSD_STATS_RC_HITS), "miss" (NFSD_STATS_RC_MISSES), or "nocache" (NFSD_STATS_RC_NOCACHE -- uncacheable)

entities.name: nfs.server.rpc.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server RPCs handled.
entities.note:
Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel's svc_stat.rpccnt, the count of good RPCs. This metric can have
an error.type of "format", "auth", or "client" for svc_stat.badfmt, svc_stat.badauth, and svc_stat.badclnt.

entities.name: nfs.server.thread.count
entities.brief: Reports the count of kernel NFS server available threads.
entities.note: Linux: this metric is taken from the Linux kernel nfsd_th_cnt variable

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.max
entities.brief: Event loop maximum delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.max` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.mean
entities.brief: Event loop mean delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.mean` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.min
entities.brief: Event loop minimum delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.min` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.p50
entities.brief: Event loop 50 percentile delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.percentile(50)` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.p90
entities.brief: Event loop 90 percentile delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.percentile(90)` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.p99
entities.brief: Event loop 99 percentile delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.percentile(99)` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.delay.stddev
entities.brief: Event loop standard deviation delay.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `histogram.stddev` of [`perf_hooks.monitorEventLoopDelay([options])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#perf_hooksmonitoreventloopdelayoptions)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.state
entities.brief: The state of event loop time.

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.time
entities.brief: Cumulative duration of time the event loop has been in each state.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from [`performance.eventLoopUtilization([utilization1[, utilization2]])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#performanceeventlooputilizationutilization1-utilization2)

entities.name: nodejs.eventloop.utilization
entities.brief: Event loop utilization.
entities.note: The value range is [0.0, 1.0] and can be retrieved from [`performance.eventLoopUtilization([utilization1[, utilization2]])`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#performanceeventlooputilizationutilization1-utilization2)

entities.name: oci.manifest.digest
entities.brief: The digest of the OCI image manifest. For container images specifically is the digest by which the container image is known.
entities.note:
Follows [OCI Image Manifest Specification](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/manifest.md), and specifically the [Digest property](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/descriptor.md#digests).
An example can be found in [Example Image Manifest](https://github.com/opencontainers/image-spec/blob/main/manifest.md#example-image-manifest).

entities.brief: ONC/Sun RPC procedure number.

entities.name: openai.api.type
entities.brief: The type of OpenAI API being used.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).

entities.name: openai.inference.client
entities.brief: Semantic Conventions for [OpenAI](https://openai.com/) client spans extend and override the semantic conventions for [Gen AI Spans](gen-ai-spans.md).
entities.note:
`gen_ai.provider.name` MUST be set to `"openai"` and SHOULD be provided **at span creation time**.

**Span name** SHOULD be `{gen_ai.operation.name} {gen_ai.request.model}`.

deprecation.note: Moved to the [OpenTelemetry GenAI semantic conventions repository](https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions-genai).
