entities.name: rpc.jsonrpc.version
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `jsonrpc.protocol.version` instead.

entities.name: rpc.message
entities.brief: Describes a message sent or received within the context of an RPC call.
entities.note: In the lifetime of an RPC stream, an event for each message sent/received on client and server spans SHOULD be created. In case of unary calls message events SHOULD NOT be recorded.

deprecation.note: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.message.compressed_size
entities.brief: Compressed size of the message in bytes.

deprecation.note: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.message.id
entities.brief: MUST be calculated as two different counters starting from `1` one for sent messages and one for received message.
entities.note: This way we guarantee that the values will be consistent between different implementations.

deprecation.note: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.message.type
entities.brief: Whether this is a received or sent message.

deprecation.note: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.message.uncompressed_size
entities.brief: Uncompressed size of the message in bytes.

deprecation.note: Deprecated, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.method
entities.brief: The fully-qualified logical name of the method from the RPC interface perspective.
entities.note:
The method name MAY have unbounded cardinality in edge or error cases.

Some RPC frameworks or libraries provide a fixed set of recognized methods
for client stubs and server implementations. Instrumentations for such
frameworks MUST set this attribute to the original method name only
when the method is recognized by the framework or library.

When the method is not recognized, for example, when the server receives
a request for a method that is not predefined on the server, or when
instrumentation is not able to reliably detect if the method is predefined,
the attribute MUST be set to `_OTHER`. In such cases, tracing
instrumentations MUST also set `rpc.method_original` attribute to
the original method value.

If the RPC instrumentation could end up converting valid RPC methods to
`_OTHER`, then it SHOULD provide a way to configure the list of recognized
RPC methods.

The `rpc.method` can be different from the name of any implementing
method/function.
The `code.function.name` attribute may be used to record the fully-qualified
method actually executing the call on the server side, or the
RPC client stub method on the client side.

entities.name: rpc.method_original
entities.brief: The original name of the method used by the client.

entities.name: rpc.request.metadata
entities.brief: RPC request metadata, `<key>` being the normalized RPC metadata key (lowercase), the value being the metadata values.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD require an explicit configuration of which metadata values are to be captured.
Including all request metadata values can be a security risk - explicit configuration helps avoid leaking sensitive information.

For example, a property `my-custom-key` with value `["1.2.3.4", "1.2.3.5"]` SHOULD be recorded as
`rpc.request.metadata.my-custom-key` attribute with value `["1.2.3.4", "1.2.3.5"]`

entities.name: rpc.response.metadata
entities.brief: RPC response metadata, `<key>` being the normalized RPC metadata key (lowercase), the value being the metadata values.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD require an explicit configuration of which metadata values are to be captured.
Including all response metadata values can be a security risk - explicit configuration helps avoid leaking sensitive information.

For example, a property `my-custom-key` with value `["attribute_value"]` SHOULD be recorded as
the `rpc.response.metadata.my-custom-key` attribute with value `["attribute_value"]`

entities.name: rpc.response.status_code
entities.brief: Status code of the RPC returned by the RPC server or generated by the client
entities.note:
Usually it represents an error code, but may also represent partial success, warning, or differentiate between various types of successful outcomes.
Semantic conventions for individual RPC frameworks SHOULD document what `rpc.response.status_code` means in the context of that system and which values are considered to represent errors.

entities.name: rpc.server
entities.brief: This span represents an incoming Remote Procedure Call (RPC).
entities.note:
**Span name:** refer to the [Span Name](/docs/rpc/rpc-spans.md#span-name) section.

**Span kind** MUST be `SERVER`.

entities.name: rpc.server.call.duration
entities.brief: Measures the duration of an incoming Remote Procedure Call (RPC).
entities.note:
When this metric is reported alongside an RPC server span, the metric value
SHOULD be the same as the RPC server span duration.

entities.name: rpc.server.call.exception
entities.brief: This event represents an exception that occurred during incoming RPC call processing, such as application errors, internal failures, or other exceptions that prevent the server from successfully handling the call.
entities.note:
This event SHOULD be recorded when an exception occurs during RPC server call processing.
Instrumentations SHOULD set the severity to ERROR (severity number 17) when recording this event.
Instrumentations MAY provide a configuration option to populate exception events with the attributes captured on the corresponding RPC server span.

entities.name: rpc.server.duration
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `rpc.server.call.duration` instead. Note: the unit also changed from `ms` to `s`.
entities.note:
While streaming RPCs may record this metric as start-of-batch
to end-of-batch, it's hard to interpret in practice.

**Streaming**: N/A.

entities.name: rpc.server.request.size
entities.brief: Measures the size of RPC request messages (uncompressed).
entities.note: **Streaming**: Recorded per message in a streaming batch

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.server.requests_per_rpc
entities.brief: Measures the number of messages received per RPC.
entities.note:
Should be 1 for all non-streaming RPCs.

**Streaming** : This metric is required for server and client streaming RPCs

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.server.response.size
entities.brief: Measures the size of RPC response messages (uncompressed).
entities.note: **Streaming**: Recorded per response in a streaming batch

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.server.responses_per_rpc
entities.brief: Measures the number of messages sent per RPC.
entities.note:
Should be 1 for all non-streaming RPCs.

**Streaming**: This metric is required for server and client streaming RPCs

deprecation.note: Removed, no replacement at this time.

entities.name: rpc.service
entities.brief: Deprecated, use fully-qualified `rpc.method` instead.

deprecation.note: Value should be included in `rpc.method` which is expected to be a fully-qualified name.

entities.name: rpc.system
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `rpc.system.name` attribute instead.

entities.name: rpc.system.name
entities.brief: The Remote Procedure Call (RPC) system.
entities.note: The client and server RPC systems may differ for the same RPC interaction. For example, a client may use Apache Dubbo or Connect RPC to communicate with a server that uses gRPC since both protocols provide compatibility with gRPC.

entities.name: security_rule.category
entities.brief: A categorization value keyword used by the entity using the rule for detection of this event

entities.name: security_rule.description
entities.brief: The description of the rule generating the event.
examples: Block requests to public DNS over HTTPS / TLS protocols

entities.name: security_rule.license
entities.brief: Name of the license under which the rule used to generate this event is made available.

entities.name: security_rule.name
entities.brief: The name of the rule or signature generating the event.

entities.name: security_rule.reference
entities.brief: Reference URL to additional information about the rule used to generate this event.
entities.note: The URL can point to the vendor’s documentation about the rule. If that’s not available, it can also be a link to a more general page describing this type of alert.

entities.name: security_rule.ruleset.name
entities.brief: Name of the ruleset, policy, group, or parent category in which the rule used to generate this event is a member.

entities.name: security_rule.uuid
entities.brief: A rule ID that is unique within the scope of a set or group of agents, observers, or other entities using the rule for detection of this event.

entities.name: security_rule.version
entities.brief: The version / revision of the rule being used for analysis.

entities.name: server.address
entities.brief: Server domain name if available without reverse DNS lookup; otherwise, IP address or Unix domain socket name.
entities.note: When observed from the client side, and when communicating through an intermediary, `server.address` SHOULD represent the server address behind any intermediaries, for example proxies, if it's available.

entities.name: server.port
entities.brief: Server port number.
entities.note: When observed from the client side, and when communicating through an intermediary, `server.port` SHOULD represent the server port behind any intermediaries, for example proxies, if it's available.

entities.name: service
entities.brief: A logical unit of an application or system that performs a specific function.
entities.note: A service is a logical component used in a system, product or application. Examples include a microservice, a database, a Kubernetes deployment.

entities.name: service_experimental
entities.brief: A service instance.

entities.name: service.criticality
entities.brief: The operational criticality of the service.
entities.note: Application developers are encouraged to set `service.criticality` to express the operational importance of their services. Telemetry consumers MAY use this attribute to optimize telemetry collection or improve user experience.

entities.name: service.instance
entities.brief: A unique instance of a logical service.
entities.note: A `service.instance` uniquely identifies an instance of a logical service. For example, a container that is part of a Kubernetes deployment that offers a service.

entities.name: service.instance.id
entities.brief: The string ID of the service instance.
entities.note:
MUST be unique for each instance of the same `service.namespace,service.name` pair (in other words
`service.namespace,service.name,service.instance.id` triplet MUST be globally unique). The ID helps to
distinguish instances of the same service that exist at the same time (e.g. instances of a horizontally scaled
service).

Implementations, such as SDKs, are recommended to generate a random Version 1 or Version 4 [RFC
4122](https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc4122.txt) UUID, but are free to use an inherent unique ID as the source of
this value if stability is desirable. In that case, the ID SHOULD be used as source of a UUID Version 5 and
SHOULD use the following UUID as the namespace: `4d63009a-8d0f-11ee-aad7-4c796ed8e320`.

UUIDs are typically recommended, as only an opaque value for the purposes of identifying a service instance is
needed. Similar to what can be seen in the man page for the
[`/etc/machine-id`](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/latest/machine-id.html) file, the underlying
data, such as pod name and namespace should be treated as confidential, being the user's choice to expose it
or not via another resource attribute.

For applications running behind an application server (like unicorn), we do not recommend using one identifier
for all processes participating in the application. Instead, it's recommended each division (e.g. a worker
thread in unicorn) to have its own instance.id.

It's not recommended for a Collector to set `service.instance.id` if it can't unambiguously determine the
service instance that is generating that telemetry. For instance, creating an UUID based on `pod.name` will
likely be wrong, as the Collector might not know from which container within that pod the telemetry originated.
However, Collectors can set the `service.instance.id` if they can unambiguously determine the service instance
for that telemetry. This is typically the case for scraping receivers, as they know the target address and
port.

entities.name: service.name
entities.brief: Logical name of the service.
entities.note:
MUST be the same for all instances of horizontally scaled services. If the value was not specified, SDKs MUST fallback to `unknown_service:` concatenated with the process executable name, e.g. `unknown_service:bash`. If the process executable name is not available, the value MUST be set to `unknown_service`.
The process executable name is the name of the process executable, the same value as described by the [`process.executable.name`](process.md) resource attribute.

entities.name: service.namespace
entities.brief: A namespace for `service.name`.
entities.note: A string value having a meaning that helps to distinguish a group of services, for example the team name that owns a group of services. `service.name` is expected to be unique within the same namespace. If `service.namespace` is not specified in the Resource then `service.name` is expected to be unique for all services that have no explicit namespace defined (so the empty/unspecified namespace is simply one more valid namespace). Zero-length namespace string is assumed equal to unspecified namespace.

entities.name: service.namespace
entities.brief: Groups related services that compose a system or application under a common namespace.
entities.note: A `service.namespace` can be used to logically organize and group related services under a common namespace.

entities.name: service.peer.name
entities.brief: Logical name of the service on the other side of the connection. SHOULD be equal to the actual [`service.name`](/docs/resource/README.md#service) resource attribute of the remote service if any.

entities.name: service.peer.namespace
entities.brief: Logical namespace of the service on the other side of the connection. SHOULD be equal to the actual [`service.namespace`](/docs/resource/README.md#service) resource attribute of the remote service if any.

entities.name: service.version
entities.brief: The version string of the service component. The format is not defined by these conventions.

entities.name: session.end
entities.brief: Indicates that a session has ended.
entities.note: For instrumentation that tracks user behavior during user sessions, a `session.end` event SHOULD be emitted every time a session ends. When a session ends and continues as a new session, this event SHOULD be emitted prior to the `session.start` event.

entities.name: session.id
entities.brief: A unique id to identify a session.

entities.name: session.previous_id
entities.brief: The previous `session.id` for this user, when known.

entities.name: session.start
entities.brief: Indicates that a new session has been started, optionally linking to the prior session.
entities.note:
For instrumentation that tracks user behavior during user sessions, a `session.start` event MUST be emitted every time a session is created. When a new session is created as a continuation of a prior session, the `session.previous_id` SHOULD be included in the event. The values of `session.id` and `session.previous_id` MUST be different.
When the `session.start` event contains both `session.id` and `session.previous_id` fields, the event indicates that the previous session has ended. If the session ID in `session.previous_id` has not yet ended via explicit `session.end` event, then the consumer SHOULD treat this continuation event as semantically equivalent to `session.end(session.previous_id)` and `session.start(session.id)`.

entities.name: signalr.connection.status
entities.brief: SignalR HTTP connection closure status.

entities.name: signalr.server.active_connections
entities.brief: Number of connections that are currently active on the server.
entities.note: Meter name: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.Connections`; Added in: ASP.NET Core 8.0

entities.name: signalr.server.connection.duration
entities.brief: The duration of connections on the server.
entities.note: Meter name: `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.Connections`; Added in: ASP.NET Core 8.0

entities.name: signalr.transport
entities.brief: [SignalR transport type](https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/main/src/SignalR/docs/specs/TransportProtocols.md)

entities.name: source.address
entities.brief: Source address - domain name if available without reverse DNS lookup; otherwise, IP address or Unix domain socket name.
entities.note: When observed from the destination side, and when communicating through an intermediary, `source.address` SHOULD represent the source address behind any intermediaries, for example proxies, if it's available.

entities.name: source.port
entities.brief: Source port number

entities.name: state
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `db.client.connection.state` instead.

entities.name: system.cpu.frequency
entities.brief: Operating frequency of the logical CPU in Hertz.

entities.name: system.cpu.logical_number
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `cpu.logical_number` instead.

entities.name: system.cpu.logical.count
entities.brief: Reports the number of logical (virtual) processor cores created by the operating system to manage multitasking.
entities.note: Calculated by multiplying the number of sockets by the number of cores per socket, and then by the number of threads per core

entities.name: system.cpu.physical.count
entities.brief: Reports the number of actual physical processor cores on the hardware.
entities.note: Calculated by multiplying the number of sockets by the number of cores per socket

entities.name: system.cpu.state
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `cpu.mode` instead.

entities.name: system.cpu.time
entities.brief: Seconds each logical CPU spent on each mode.

entities.name: system.cpu.utilization
entities.brief: For each logical CPU, the utilization is calculated as the change in cumulative CPU time (cpu.time) over a measurement interval, divided by the elapsed time.

entities.name: system.device
entities.brief: The device identifier

entities.name: system.disk.io
entities.brief: Disk bytes transferred.

entities.name: system.disk.io_time
entities.brief: Time disk spent activated.
entities.note:
The real elapsed time ("wall clock") used in the I/O path (time from operations running in parallel are not counted). Measured as:

- Linux: Field 13 from [procfs-diskstats](https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/ABI/testing/procfs-diskstats)
- Windows: The complement of
  ["Disk\% Idle Time"](https://learn.microsoft.com/archive/blogs/askcore/windows-performance-monitor-disk-counters-explained#windows-performance-monitor-disk-counters-explained)
  performance counter: `uptime * (100 - "Disk\% Idle Time") / 100`

entities.name: system.disk.limit
entities.brief: The total storage capacity of the disk.

entities.name: system.disk.merged
entities.brief: The number of disk reads/writes merged into single physical disk access operations.

entities.name: system.disk.operation_time
entities.brief: Sum of the time each operation took to complete.
entities.note:
Because it is the sum of time each request took, parallel-issued requests each contribute to make the count grow. Measured as:

- Linux: Fields 7 & 11 from [procfs-diskstats](https://www.kernel.org/doc/Documentation/ABI/testing/procfs-diskstats)
- Windows: "Avg. Disk sec/Read" perf counter multiplied by "Disk Reads/sec" perf counter (similar for Writes)

entities.name: system.disk.operations
entities.brief: Disk operations count.

entities.name: system.filesystem.limit
entities.brief: The total storage capacity of the filesystem.

entities.name: system.filesystem.lock.count
entities.brief: Filesystem lock counts.

entities.name: system.filesystem.mode
entities.brief: The filesystem mode

entities.name: system.filesystem.mountpoint
entities.brief: The filesystem mount path

entities.name: system.filesystem.state
entities.brief: The filesystem state

entities.name: system.filesystem.type
entities.brief: The filesystem type

entities.name: system.filesystem.usage
entities.brief: Reports a filesystem's space usage across different states.
entities.note:
The sum of all `system.filesystem.usage` values over the different `system.filesystem.state` attributes
SHOULD equal the total storage capacity of the filesystem, that is `system.filesystem.limit`.

entities.name: system.filesystem.utilization
entities.brief: Fraction of filesystem bytes used.

entities.name: system.linux.memory.available
entities.brief: The number of packets transferred.

entities.name: system.linux.memory.slab.usage
entities.brief: The number of packets transferred.

entities.name: system.memory.limit
entities.brief: Total virtual memory available in the system.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.available
entities.brief: An estimate of how much memory is available for starting new applications, without causing swapping.
entities.note:
This is an alternative to `system.memory.usage` metric with `state=free`.
Linux starting from 3.14 exports "available" memory. It takes "free" memory as a baseline, and then factors in kernel-specific values.
This is supposed to be more accurate than just "free" memory.
For reference, see the calculations [here](https://superuser.com/a/980821).
See also `MemAvailable` in [/proc/meminfo](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html).

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.limit
entities.brief: Total number of hugepages available.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.page_size
entities.brief: System hugepage size in bytes.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.reserved
entities.brief: Number of reserved hugepages.
entities.note:
Hugepages for which a commitment to allocate has been made, but no allocation has yet been made.
This is reported as a separate metric rather than a `usage` state because reserved pages are already counted in `free` pages.
They represent a subset of free pages that cannot be used for non-reserved allocations.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.state
entities.brief: The Linux HugePages memory state

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.surplus
entities.brief: Number of surplus hugepages.
entities.note:
Overcommitted hugepages beyond the persistent pool.
This is reported as a separate metric rather than a `usage` state because surplus pages can be in either `used` or `free` state.
Including them in `usage` would break the convention that `usage` states sum to the `limit`.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.usage
entities.brief: Number of hugepages in use by state.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.hugepages.utilization
entities.brief: Percentage of hugepages in use by state.

entities.name: system.memory.linux.shared
entities.brief: Shared memory used (mostly by tmpfs).
entities.note:
Equivalent of `shared` from [`free` command](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man1/free.1.html) or
`Shmem` from [`/proc/meminfo`](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html)"

entities.name: system.memory.linux.slab.state
entities.brief: The Linux Slab memory state

entities.name: system.memory.linux.slab.usage
entities.brief: Reports the memory used by the Linux kernel for managing caches of frequently used objects.
entities.note:
The sum over the `reclaimable` and `unreclaimable` state values in `memory.linux.slab.usage` SHOULD be equal to the total slab memory available on the system.
Note that the total slab memory is not constant and may vary over time.
See also the [Slab allocator](https://blogs.oracle.com/linux/post/understanding-linux-kernel-memory-statistics) and `Slab` in [/proc/meminfo](https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man5/proc.5.html).

entities.name: system.memory.shared
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `system.memory.linux.shared` instead.

entities.name: system.memory.state
entities.brief: The memory state

entities.name: system.memory.usage
entities.brief: Reports memory in use by state.

entities.name: system.memory.utilization
entities.brief: Percentage of memory bytes in use.

entities.name: system.network.connection.count
entities.brief: The number of connections.

entities.name: system.network.connections
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `system.network.connection.count` instead.

entities.name: system.network.dropped
entities.brief: Count of packets that are dropped or discarded even though there was no error.
entities.note:
Measured as:

- Linux: the `drop` column in `/proc/dev/net` ([source](https://web.archive.org/web/20180321091318/http://www.onlamp.com/pub/a/linux/2000/11/16/LinuxAdmin.html))
- Windows: [`InDiscards`/`OutDiscards`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/ns-netioapi-mib_if_row2)
  from [`GetIfEntry2`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/nf-netioapi-getifentry2)

entities.name: system.network.errors
entities.brief: Count of network errors detected.
entities.note:
Measured as:

- Linux: the `errs` column in `/proc/net/dev` ([source](https://web.archive.org/web/20180321091318/http://www.onlamp.com/pub/a/linux/2000/11/16/LinuxAdmin.html)).
- Windows: [`InErrors`/`OutErrors`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/ns-netioapi-mib_if_row2)
  from [`GetIfEntry2`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/nf-netioapi-getifentry2).

entities.name: system.network.io
entities.brief: The number of bytes transmitted and received.

entities.name: system.network.packet.count
entities.brief: The number of packets transferred.

entities.name: system.network.packet.dropped
entities.brief: Count of packets that are dropped or discarded even though there was no error.
entities.note:
Measured as:

- Linux: the `drop` column in `/proc/net/dev` ([source](https://web.archive.org/web/20180321091318/http://www.onlamp.com/pub/a/linux/2000/11/16/LinuxAdmin.html))
- Windows: [`InDiscards`/`OutDiscards`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/ns-netioapi-mib_if_row2)
  from [`GetIfEntry2`](https://docs.microsoft.com/windows/win32/api/netioapi/nf-netioapi-getifentry2)

entities.name: system.network.packets
entities.brief: The number of packets transferred.

entities.name: system.network.state
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `network.connection.state` instead.

entities.name: system.paging.direction
entities.brief: The paging access direction

entities.name: system.paging.fault.type
entities.brief: The paging fault type

entities.name: system.paging.faults
entities.brief: The number of page faults.

entities.name: system.paging.operations
entities.brief: The number of paging operations.

entities.name: system.paging.state
entities.brief: The memory paging state

entities.name: system.paging.type
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `system.paging.fault.type` instead.

entities.name: system.paging.usage
entities.brief: Unix swap or windows pagefile usage.

entities.name: system.paging.utilization
entities.brief: Swap (unix) or pagefile (windows) utilization.

entities.name: system.process.count
entities.brief: Total number of processes in each state.

entities.name: system.process.created
entities.brief: Total number of processes created over uptime of the host.

entities.name: system.process.status
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `process.state` instead.

entities.name: system.processes.status
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `process.state` instead.

entities.name: system.uptime
entities.brief: The time the system has been running.
entities.note:
Instrumentations SHOULD use a gauge with type `double` and measure uptime in seconds as a floating point number with the highest precision available.
The actual accuracy would depend on the instrumentation and operating system.

entities.name: telemetry
entities.brief: The telemetry SDK used to capture data recorded by the instrumentation libraries.

entities.name: telemetry_experimental
entities.brief: The telemetry SDK used to capture data recorded by the instrumentation libraries.

entities.name: telemetry.distro
entities.brief: The distribution of telemetry SDK used to capture data recorded by the instrumentation libraries.

entities.name: telemetry.distro.name
entities.brief: The name of the auto instrumentation agent or distribution, if used.
entities.note:
Official auto instrumentation agents and distributions SHOULD set the `telemetry.distro.name` attribute to
a string starting with `opentelemetry-`, e.g. `opentelemetry-java-instrumentation`.

entities.name: telemetry.distro.version
entities.brief: The version string of the auto instrumentation agent or distribution, if used.

entities.name: telemetry.sdk
entities.brief: The telemetry SDK used to capture data recorded by the instrumentation libraries.

entities.name: telemetry.sdk.language
entities.brief: The language of the telemetry SDK.

entities.name: telemetry.sdk.name
entities.brief: The name of the telemetry SDK as defined above.
entities.note:
The OpenTelemetry SDK MUST set the `telemetry.sdk.name` attribute to `opentelemetry`.
If another SDK, like a fork or a vendor-provided implementation, is used, this SDK MUST set the
`telemetry.sdk.name` attribute to the fully-qualified class or module name of this SDK's main entry point
or another suitable identifier depending on the language.
The identifier `opentelemetry` is reserved and MUST NOT be used in this case.
All custom identifiers SHOULD be stable across different versions of an implementation.

entities.name: telemetry.sdk.version
entities.brief: The version string of the telemetry SDK.

entities.name: test.case.name
entities.brief: The fully qualified human readable name of the [test case](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Test_case).

entities.name: test.case.result.status
entities.brief: The status of the actual test case result from test execution.

entities.name: test.suite.name
entities.brief: The human readable name of a [test suite](https://wikipedia.org/wiki/Test_suite).

entities.name: test.suite.run.status
entities.brief: The status of the test suite run.

entities.name: thread
entities.brief: These attributes may be used for any operation to store information about a thread that started a span.

entities.name: thread.id
entities.brief: Current "managed" thread ID (as opposed to OS thread ID).
entities.note:
Examples of where the value can be extracted from:

| Language or platform | Source |
| --- | --- |
| JVM | `Thread.currentThread().threadId()` |
| .NET | `Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId` |
| Python | `threading.current_thread().ident` |
| Ruby | `Thread.current.object_id` |
| C++ | `std::this_thread::get_id()` |
| Erlang | `erlang:self()` |

entities.name: thread.name
entities.brief: Current thread name.
entities.note:
Examples of where the value can be extracted from:

| Language or platform | Source |
| --- | --- |
| JVM | `Thread.currentThread().getName()` |
| .NET | `Thread.CurrentThread.Name` |
| Python | `threading.current_thread().name` |
| Ruby | `Thread.current.name` |
| Erlang | `erlang:process_info(self(), registered_name)` |

entities.name: tls.cipher
entities.brief: String indicating the [cipher](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5246#appendix-A.5) used during the current connection.
entities.note: The values allowed for `tls.cipher` MUST be one of the `Descriptions` of the [registered TLS Cipher Suits](https://www.iana.org/assignments/tls-parameters/tls-parameters.xhtml#table-tls-parameters-4).

entities.name: tls.client.certificate
entities.brief: PEM-encoded stand-alone certificate offered by the client. This is usually mutually-exclusive of `client.certificate_chain` since this value also exists in that list.

entities.name: tls.client.certificate_chain
entities.brief: Array of PEM-encoded certificates that make up the certificate chain offered by the client. This is usually mutually-exclusive of `client.certificate` since that value should be the first certificate in the chain.

entities.name: tls.client.hash.md5
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the MD5 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the client. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.client.hash.sha1
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the SHA1 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the client. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.client.hash.sha256
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the SHA256 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the client. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.client.issuer
entities.brief: Distinguished name of [subject](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5280#section-4.1.2.6) of the issuer of the x.509 certificate presented by the client.
examples: CN=Example Root CA, OU=Infrastructure Team, DC=example, DC=com

entities.name: tls.client.ja3
entities.brief: A hash that identifies clients based on how they perform an SSL/TLS handshake.

entities.name: tls.client.not_after
entities.brief: Date/Time indicating when client certificate is no longer considered valid.

entities.name: tls.client.not_before
entities.brief: Date/Time indicating when client certificate is first considered valid.

entities.name: tls.client.server_name
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `server.address` instead.

entities.name: tls.client.subject
entities.brief: Distinguished name of subject of the x.509 certificate presented by the client.

entities.name: tls.client.supported_ciphers
entities.brief: Array of ciphers offered by the client during the client hello.

entities.name: tls.curve
entities.brief: String indicating the curve used for the given cipher, when applicable

entities.name: tls.established
entities.brief: Boolean flag indicating if the TLS negotiation was successful and transitioned to an encrypted tunnel.

entities.name: tls.next_protocol
entities.brief: String indicating the protocol being tunneled. Per the values in the [IANA registry](https://www.iana.org/assignments/tls-extensiontype-values/tls-extensiontype-values.xhtml#alpn-protocol-ids), this string should be lower case.

entities.name: tls.protocol.name
entities.brief: Normalized lowercase protocol name parsed from original string of the negotiated [SSL/TLS protocol version](https://docs.openssl.org/1.1.1/man3/SSL_get_version/#return-values)

entities.name: tls.protocol.version
entities.brief: Numeric part of the version parsed from the original string of the negotiated [SSL/TLS protocol version](https://docs.openssl.org/1.1.1/man3/SSL_get_version/#return-values)

entities.name: tls.resumed
entities.brief: Boolean flag indicating if this TLS connection was resumed from an existing TLS negotiation.

entities.name: tls.server.certificate
entities.brief: PEM-encoded stand-alone certificate offered by the server. This is usually mutually-exclusive of `server.certificate_chain` since this value also exists in that list.

entities.name: tls.server.certificate_chain
entities.brief: Array of PEM-encoded certificates that make up the certificate chain offered by the server. This is usually mutually-exclusive of `server.certificate` since that value should be the first certificate in the chain.

entities.name: tls.server.hash.md5
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the MD5 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the server. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.server.hash.sha1
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the SHA1 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the server. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.server.hash.sha256
entities.brief: Certificate fingerprint using the SHA256 digest of DER-encoded version of certificate offered by the server. For consistency with other hash values, this value should be formatted as an uppercase hash.

entities.name: tls.server.issuer
entities.brief: Distinguished name of [subject](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5280#section-4.1.2.6) of the issuer of the x.509 certificate presented by the client.
examples: CN=Example Root CA, OU=Infrastructure Team, DC=example, DC=com

entities.name: tls.server.ja3s
entities.brief: A hash that identifies servers based on how they perform an SSL/TLS handshake.

entities.name: tls.server.not_after
entities.brief: Date/Time indicating when server certificate is no longer considered valid.

entities.name: tls.server.not_before
entities.brief: Date/Time indicating when server certificate is first considered valid.

entities.name: tls.server.subject
entities.brief: Distinguished name of subject of the x.509 certificate presented by the server.

entities.name: trace.gen_ai.client
entities.brief: Describes GenAI operation span.

entities.name: trace.gen_ai.client.common
entities.brief: Describes GenAI operation span.

entities.name: trace.http.client
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for HTTP Client

entities.name: trace.http.server
entities.brief: Semantic Convention for HTTP Server

entities.name: url.domain
entities.brief: Domain extracted from the `url.full`, such as "opentelemetry.io".
entities.note: In some cases a URL may refer to an IP and/or port directly, without a domain name. In this case, the IP address would go to the domain field. If the URL contains a [literal IPv6 address](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2732#section-2) enclosed by `[` and `]`, the `[` and `]` characters should also be captured in the domain field.

entities.name: url.extension
entities.brief: The file extension extracted from the `url.full`, excluding the leading dot.
entities.note: The file extension is only set if it exists, as not every url has a file extension. When the file name has multiple extensions `example.tar.gz`, only the last one should be captured `gz`, not `tar.gz`.

entities.name: url.fragment
entities.brief: The [URI fragment](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.5) component

entities.name: url.full
entities.brief: Absolute URL describing a network resource according to [RFC3986](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986)
entities.note:
For network calls, URL usually has `scheme://host[:port][path][?query][#fragment]` format, where the fragment
is not transmitted over HTTP, but if it is known, it SHOULD be included nevertheless.

`url.full` MUST NOT contain credentials passed via URL in form of `https://username:password@www.example.com/`.
In such case username and password SHOULD be redacted and attribute's value SHOULD be `https://REDACTED:REDACTED@www.example.com/`.

`url.full` SHOULD capture the absolute URL when it is available (or can be reconstructed).

Sensitive content provided in `url.full` SHOULD be scrubbed when instrumentations can identify it.

![Development](https://img.shields.io/badge/-development-blue)
Query string values for the following keys SHOULD be redacted by default and replaced by the
value `REDACTED`:

* [`X-Amz-Signature`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`X-Amz-Credential`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`X-Amz-Security-Token`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`sig`](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview#sas-token)
* [`X-Goog-Signature`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/signed-urls)

This list is subject to change over time.

Matching of query parameter keys against the sensitive list SHOULD be case-sensitive.

![Development](https://img.shields.io/badge/-development-blue)
Instrumentation MAY provide a way to override this list via declarative configuration.
If so, it SHOULD use the `sensitive_query_parameters` property
(an array of case-sensitive strings with minimum items 0) under
`.instrumentation/development.general.sanitization.url`.
This list is a full override of the default sensitive query parameter keys,
it is not a list of keys in addition to the defaults.

When a query string value is redacted, the query string key SHOULD still be preserved, e.g.
`https://www.example.com/path?color=blue&sig=REDACTED`.

entities.name: url.original
entities.brief: Unmodified original URL as seen in the event source.
entities.note:
In network monitoring, the observed URL may be a full URL, whereas in access logs, the URL is often just represented as a path. This field is meant to represent the URL as it was observed, complete or not.
`url.original` might contain credentials passed via URL in form of `https://username:password@www.example.com/`. In such case password and username SHOULD NOT be redacted and attribute's value SHOULD remain the same.

entities.name: url.path
entities.brief: The [URI path](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.3) component
entities.note: Sensitive content provided in `url.path` SHOULD be scrubbed when instrumentations can identify it.

entities.name: url.port
entities.brief: Port extracted from the `url.full`

entities.name: url.query
entities.brief: The [URI query](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.4) component
entities.note:
Sensitive content provided in `url.query` SHOULD be scrubbed when instrumentations can identify it.

![Development](https://img.shields.io/badge/-development-blue)
Query string values for the following keys SHOULD be redacted by default and replaced by the value `REDACTED`:

* [`X-Amz-Signature`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`X-Amz-Credential`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`X-Amz-Security-Token`](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/reference_sigv-authentication-methods.html)
* [`sig`](https://learn.microsoft.com/azure/storage/common/storage-sas-overview#sas-token)
* [`X-Goog-Signature`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/access-control/signed-urls)

This list is subject to change over time.

Matching of query parameter keys against the sensitive list SHOULD be case-sensitive.

Instrumentation MAY provide a way to override this list via declarative configuration.
If so, it SHOULD use the `sensitive_query_parameters` property
(an array of case-sensitive strings with minimum items 0) under
`.instrumentation/development.general.sanitization.url`.
This list is a full override of the default sensitive query parameter keys,
it is not a list of keys in addition to the defaults.

When a query string value is redacted, the query string key SHOULD still be preserved, e.g.
`q=OpenTelemetry&sig=REDACTED`.

entities.name: url.registered_domain
entities.brief: The highest registered url domain, stripped of the subdomain.
entities.note: This value can be determined precisely with the [public suffix list](https://publicsuffix.org/). For example, the registered domain for `foo.example.com` is `example.com`. Trying to approximate this by simply taking the last two labels will not work well for TLDs such as `co.uk`.

entities.name: url.scheme
entities.brief: The [URI scheme](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-3.1) component identifying the used protocol.

entities.name: url.subdomain
entities.brief: The subdomain portion of a fully qualified domain name includes all of the names except the host name under the registered_domain. In a partially qualified domain, or if the qualification level of the full name cannot be determined, subdomain contains all of the names below the registered domain.
entities.note: The subdomain portion of `www.east.mydomain.co.uk` is `east`. If the domain has multiple levels of subdomain, such as `sub2.sub1.example.com`, the subdomain field should contain `sub2.sub1`, with no trailing period.

entities.name: url.template
entities.brief: The low-cardinality template of an [absolute path reference](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc3986#section-4.2).

entities.name: url.top_level_domain
entities.brief: The effective top level domain (eTLD), also known as the domain suffix, is the last part of the domain name. For example, the top level domain for example.com is `com`.
entities.note: This value can be determined precisely with the [public suffix list](https://publicsuffix.org/).

entities.name: user_agent.name
entities.brief: Name of the user-agent extracted from original. Usually refers to the browser's name.
entities.note: [Example](https://uaparser.dev/#demo) of extracting browser's name from original string. In the case of using a user-agent for non-browser products, such as microservices with multiple names/versions inside the `user_agent.original`, the most significant name SHOULD be selected. In such a scenario it should align with `user_agent.version`

entities.name: user_agent.original
entities.brief: Value of the [HTTP User-Agent](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110.html#field.user-agent) header sent by the client.
examples: Mozilla/5.0 (iPhone; CPU iPhone OS 14_7_1 like Mac OS X) AppleWebKit/605.1.15 (KHTML, like Gecko) Version/14.1.2 Mobile/15E148 Safari/604.1

entities.name: user_agent.os.name
entities.brief: Human readable operating system name.
entities.note: For mapping user agent strings to OS names, libraries such as [ua-parser](https://github.com/ua-parser) can be utilized.

entities.name: user_agent.os.version
entities.brief: The version string of the operating system as defined in [Version Attributes](/docs/resource/README.md#version-attributes).
entities.note: For mapping user agent strings to OS versions, libraries such as [ua-parser](https://github.com/ua-parser) can be utilized.

entities.name: user_agent.synthetic.type
entities.brief: Specifies the category of synthetic traffic, such as tests or bots.
entities.note: This attribute MAY be derived from the contents of the `user_agent.original` attribute. Components that populate the attribute are responsible for determining what they consider to be synthetic bot or test traffic. This attribute can either be set for self-identification purposes, or on telemetry detected to be generated as a result of a synthetic request. This attribute is useful for distinguishing between genuine client traffic and synthetic traffic generated by bots or tests.

entities.name: user_agent.version
entities.brief: Version of the user-agent extracted from original. Usually refers to the browser's version
entities.note: [Example](https://uaparser.dev/#demo) of extracting browser's version from original string. In the case of using a user-agent for non-browser products, such as microservices with multiple names/versions inside the `user_agent.original`, the most significant version SHOULD be selected. In such a scenario it should align with `user_agent.name`

entities.name: user.email
entities.brief: User email address.

entities.name: user.full_name
entities.brief: User's full name

entities.name: user.hash
entities.brief: Unique user hash to correlate information for a user in anonymized form.
entities.note: Useful if `user.id` or `user.name` contain confidential information and cannot be used.

entities.name: user.id
entities.brief: Unique identifier of the user.

entities.name: user.name
entities.brief: Short name or login/username of the user.

entities.name: user.roles
entities.brief: Array of user roles at the time of the event.

entities.name: v8js.gc.duration
entities.brief: Garbage collection duration.
entities.note: The values can be retrieved from [`perf_hooks.PerformanceObserver(...).observe({ entryTypes: ['gc'] })`](https://nodejs.org/api/perf_hooks.html#performanceobserverobserveoptions)

entities.name: v8js.gc.type
entities.brief: The type of garbage collection.

entities.name: v8js.heap.space.available_size
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `v8js.memory.heap.space.available_size` instead.

entities.name: v8js.heap.space.name
entities.brief: The name of the space type of heap memory.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `space_name` of [`v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()`](https://nodejs.org/api/v8.html#v8getheapspacestatistics)

entities.name: v8js.heap.space.physical_size
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `v8js.memory.heap.space.physical_size` instead.

entities.name: v8js.memory.heap.limit
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `v8js.memory.heap.space.size` instead.

entities.name: v8js.memory.heap.space.available_size
entities.brief: Heap space available size.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `space_available_size` of [`v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()`](https://nodejs.org/api/v8.html#v8getheapspacestatistics)

entities.name: v8js.memory.heap.space.physical_size
entities.brief: Committed size of a heap space.
entities.note: Value can be retrieved from value `physical_space_size` of [`v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()`](https://nodejs.org/api/v8.html#v8getheapspacestatistics)

entities.name: v8js.memory.heap.space.size
entities.brief: Total heap memory size pre-allocated for a heap space.
entities.note: The value can be retrieved from value `space_size` of [`v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()`](https://nodejs.org/api/v8.html#v8getheapspacestatistics)

entities.name: v8js.memory.heap.used
entities.brief: Heap Memory size allocated.
entities.note: The value can be retrieved from value `space_used_size` of [`v8.getHeapSpaceStatistics()`](https://nodejs.org/api/v8.html#v8getheapspacestatistics)

entities.name: v8js.resource.active
entities.brief: Gauge of the active resources that are currently keeping the event loop alive.
entities.note: The values can be retrieved from [`process.getActiveResourcesInfo()`](https://nodejs.org/api/process.html#processgetactiveresourcesinfo)

entities.name: v8js.resource.type
entities.brief: The type of resource keeping the event loop active.

entities.name: vcs.change.count
entities.brief: The number of changes (pull requests/merge requests/changelists) in a repository, categorized by their state (e.g. open or merged).

entities.name: vcs.change.duration
entities.brief: The time duration a change (pull request/merge request/changelist) has been in a given state.

entities.name: vcs.change.id
entities.brief: The ID of the change (pull request/merge request/changelist) if applicable. This is usually a unique (within repository) identifier generated by the VCS system.

entities.name: vcs.change.state
entities.brief: The state of the change (pull request/merge request/changelist).

entities.name: vcs.change.time_to_approval
entities.brief: The amount of time since its creation it took a change (pull request/merge request/changelist) to get the first approval.

entities.name: vcs.change.time_to_merge
entities.brief: The amount of time since its creation it took a change (pull request/merge request/changelist) to get merged into the target(base) ref.

entities.name: vcs.change.title
entities.brief: The human readable title of the change (pull request/merge request/changelist). This title is often a brief summary of the change and may get merged in to a ref as the commit summary.

entities.name: vcs.contributor.count
entities.brief: The number of unique contributors to a repository.

entities.name: vcs.line_change.type
entities.brief: The type of line change being measured on a branch or change.

entities.name: vcs.owner.name
entities.brief: The group owner within the version control system.

entities.name: vcs.provider.name
entities.brief: The name of the version control system provider.

entities.name: vcs.ref
entities.brief: A reference to a specific version in the Version Control System.

entities.name: vcs.ref.base.name
entities.brief: The name of the [reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitglossary#def_ref) such as **branch** or **tag** in the repository.
entities.note:
`base` refers to the starting point of a change. For example, `main`
would be the base reference of type branch if you've created a new
reference of type branch from it and created new commits.

entities.name: vcs.ref.base.revision
entities.brief: The revision, literally [revised version](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/revision), The revision most often refers to a commit object in Git, or a revision number in SVN.
entities.note:
`base` refers to the starting point of a change. For example, `main`
would be the base reference of type branch if you've created a new
reference of type branch from it and created new commits. The
revision can be a full [hash value (see
glossary)](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/FIPS/NIST.FIPS.186-5.pdf),
of the recorded change to a ref within a repository pointing to a
commit [commit](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit) object. It does
not necessarily have to be a hash; it can simply define a [revision
number](https://svnbook.red-bean.com/en/1.7/svn.tour.revs.specifiers.html)
which is an integer that is monotonically increasing. In cases where
it is identical to the `ref.base.name`, it SHOULD still be included.
It is up to the implementer to decide which value to set as the
revision based on the VCS system and situational context.

entities.name: vcs.ref.base.type
entities.brief: The type of the [reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitglossary#def_ref) in the repository.
entities.note:
`base` refers to the starting point of a change. For example, `main`
would be the base reference of type branch if you've created a new
reference of type branch from it and created new commits.

entities.name: vcs.ref.count
entities.brief: The number of refs of type branch or tag in a repository.

entities.name: vcs.ref.head.name
entities.brief: The name of the [reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitglossary#def_ref) such as **branch** or **tag** in the repository.
entities.note:
`head` refers to where you are right now; the current reference at a
given time.

entities.name: vcs.ref.head.revision
entities.brief: The revision, literally [revised version](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/revision), The revision most often refers to a commit object in Git, or a revision number in SVN.
entities.note:
`head` refers to where you are right now; the current reference at a
given time.The revision can be a full [hash value (see
glossary)](https://nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/FIPS/NIST.FIPS.186-5.pdf),
of the recorded change to a ref within a repository pointing to a
commit [commit](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-commit) object. It does
not necessarily have to be a hash; it can simply define a [revision
number](https://svnbook.red-bean.com/en/1.7/svn.tour.revs.specifiers.html)
which is an integer that is monotonically increasing. In cases where
it is identical to the `ref.head.name`, it SHOULD still be included.
It is up to the implementer to decide which value to set as the
revision based on the VCS system and situational context.

entities.name: vcs.ref.head.type
entities.brief: The type of the [reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitglossary#def_ref) in the repository.
entities.note:
`head` refers to where you are right now; the current reference at a
given time.

entities.name: vcs.ref.lines_delta
entities.brief: The number of lines added/removed in a ref (branch) relative to the ref from the `vcs.ref.base.name` attribute.
entities.note:
This metric should be reported for each `vcs.line_change.type` value. For example if a ref added 3 lines and removed 2 lines,
instrumentation SHOULD report two measurements: 3 and 2 (both positive numbers).
If number of lines added/removed should be calculated from the start of time, then `vcs.ref.base.name` SHOULD be set to an empty string.

entities.name: vcs.ref.revisions_delta
entities.brief: The number of revisions (commits) a ref (branch) is ahead/behind the branch from the `vcs.ref.base.name` attribute.
entities.note:
This metric should be reported for each `vcs.revision_delta.direction` value. For example if branch `a` is 3 commits behind and 2 commits ahead of `trunk`,
instrumentation SHOULD report two measurements: 3 and 2 (both positive numbers) and `vcs.ref.base.name` is set to `trunk`.

entities.name: vcs.ref.time
entities.brief: Time a ref (branch) created from the default branch (trunk) has existed. The `ref.type` attribute will always be `branch`.

entities.name: vcs.ref.type
entities.brief: The type of the [reference](https://git-scm.com/docs/gitglossary#def_ref) in the repository.

entities.name: vcs.repo
entities.brief: A repository in the Version Control System.

entities.name: vcs.repository
entities.brief: A repository in the Version Control System.

entities.name: vcs.repository.change.id
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `vcs.change.id` instead.

entities.name: vcs.repository.change.title
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `vcs.change.title` instead.

entities.name: vcs.repository.count
entities.brief: The number of repositories in an organization.

entities.name: vcs.repository.name
entities.brief: The human readable name of the repository. It SHOULD NOT include any additional identifier like Group/SubGroup in GitLab or organization in GitHub.
entities.note:
Due to it only being the name, it can clash with forks of the same
repository if collecting telemetry across multiple orgs or groups in
the same backends.

entities.name: vcs.repository.ref.name
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `vcs.ref.head.name` instead.

entities.name: vcs.repository.ref.revision
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `vcs.ref.head.revision` instead.

entities.name: vcs.repository.ref.type
entities.brief: Deprecated, use `vcs.ref.head.type` instead.

entities.name: vcs.repository.url.full
entities.brief: The [canonical URL](https://support.google.com/webmasters/answer/10347851) of the repository providing the complete HTTP(S) address in order to locate and identify the repository through a browser.
entities.note:
In Git Version Control Systems, the canonical URL SHOULD NOT include
the `.git` extension.

entities.name: vcs.revision_delta.direction
entities.brief: The type of revision comparison.

entities.name: webengine
entities.brief: Resource describing the packaged software running the application code. Web engines are typically executed using process.runtime.

entities.name: webengine_resource
entities.brief: Resource describing the packaged software running the application code. Web engines are typically executed using process.runtime.

entities.name: webengine.description
entities.brief: Additional description of the web engine (e.g. detailed version and edition information).

entities.name: webengine.name
entities.brief: The name of the web engine.

entities.name: webengine.version
entities.brief: The version of the web engine.

entities.name: zos.smf.id
entities.brief: The System Management Facility (SMF) Identifier uniquely identified a z/OS system within a SYSPLEX or mainframe environment and is used for system and performance analysis.

entities.name: zos.software
entities.brief: A software resource running on a z/OS system.

entities.name: zos.sysplex.name
entities.brief: The name of the SYSPLEX to which the z/OS system belongs too.
